title: Replace last-write-wins cache with per-field LWW-register CRDT for offline note editing
business_context: A field-service inspection app (React PWA) is used by 12,000 technicians on flaky cellular networks. Inspectors edit multi-section reports offline; when two devices edit the same report the current sync silently overwrites the whole document, destroying 3-8% of inspections per week (~900 lost reports). Each lost report means a re-visit costing ~$140.
constraints: Must merge concurrent edits at field granularity, not document granularity
Clients can be offline for up to 14 days; clock skew up to 5 minutes
Existing 2.1M documents in IndexedDB must keep working without a re-download
Sync payload per report must stay under 64KB to fit cellular budgets
No central locking; server is stateless behind 6 instances

before_state.description: Sync sends the whole document with a single updatedAt; server keeps the newest timestamp and discards the other, losing concurrent field edits.

issues: Whole-document LWW discards concurrent edits to different fields
force:true overwrites server state, deleting the other device's work
updatedAt comparison is wall-clock and breaks under 5min skew
No per-field timestamps so merges cannot be field-scoped
rev is a single integer, cannot represent concurrent branches

reasoning: Naive approach: keep whole-doc LWW but add a 'merge on 409' that picks newest doc - rejected because it still loses the losing device's fields entirely and skew makes 'newest' wrong. Alternative considered: full Automerge/Yjs CRDT - rejected because the 2.1M existing docs would need re-encoding and the op-log inflates payloads past the 64KB cellular budget. Chosen path: a per-field LWW-Register (value + HLC timestamp + nodeId) which is O(fields) to merge and tiny on the wire. Cross-layer trace: UI marks a field dirty -> writes {value, hlc} into IndexedDB -> pushDoc sends only dirty field registers -> server merges register-by-register taking max(hlc) with nodeId tiebreak -> returns merged registers -> client applies -> migration backfills hlc for legacy docs lazily on first edit -> tests assert concurrent edits to different fields both survive.

after_state.description: Each field is an LWW-Register with a Hybrid Logical Clock; merges are commutative and field-scoped, so concurrent edits to different fields both survive and skew is bounded by HLC.

files.purpose: Provides a clock that is monotonic and total-ordered despite 5min skew, giving deterministic field merges.
Field-scoped commutative/idempotent merge so concurrent edits never clobber each other.
Sends only dirty field registers (<64KB) and re-merges the authoritative response idempotently.
Stateless server merge inside a tx; commutativity means the 6 instances need no locking.

migration_strategy: Ship HLC+register code dark behind flag crdt_merge=off; legacy LWW path stays default
Lazy-backfill: on first edit of a legacy doc, wrap each scalar field in {v, t:now()} and persist
Enable crdt_merge for 1% of technicians (canary), compare lost-report metric vs control cohort
Ramp 1%->10%->50%->100% over 2 weeks watching merge-conflict and payload-size dashboards
Remove force:true legacy branch and old updatedAt column once 100% on CRDT for 30 days
Rollback at any stage by flipping flag; legacy docs untouched, CRDT docs still readable as scalars
testing_strategy: Fixture: two DocCrdt for the same report where device A edits fields.notes and device B edits fields.signature concurrently (interleaved HLCs). Assert mergeDoc output contains BOTH edits (output assertion) and that store.put was called once with the union (side-effect via tx spy). Property test: mergeDoc(a,mergeDoc(b,c)) == mergeDoc(mergeDoc(a,b),c) over 1000 random op sets. Chaos test: inject 5min clock skew on device B and a duplicated POST /merge (retry); assert final state is identical and no field is lost (idempotence).
what_breaks_naive_approach: At the 409 boundary the naive code chooses an entire document by wall-clock updatedAt; under skew the device with the wrong clock 'wins' and force:true erases the other device's field edits entirely - exactly the 3-8% weekly loss.

title: Add idempotency keys and outbox dedup to a Background Sync order queue to stop duplicate charges
business_context: A coffee-chain ordering PWA (Next.js) lets 80,000 daily customers place orders that complete even when they walk into a dead-zone. The Background Sync replays the queued POST when connectivity returns, but the service worker retries on every 'sync' event, double-charging ~0.6% of orders (~480/day) and triggering chargebacks averaging $9 plus support time.
constraints: Exactly-once charge semantics across SW retries and tab reloads
Service worker can be killed mid-replay at any await point
Payment API is at-least-once and may itself retry
IndexedDB outbox must survive app updates and 7-day offline windows
No server-side session affinity; 4 API pods behind a load balancer

before_state.description: The sync handler reads the outbox and POSTs each order with no idempotency key; if the SW is killed after the POST but before deleting the row, the next sync re-POSTs and double-charges.

issues: No idempotency key, so a retried POST creates a second order+charge
delete happens after fetch; a crash between them re-sends
getAll then loop has no claim, so two concurrent sync events double-process
Server has no dedup table; identical retried bodies charge twice
payments.charge is itself at-least-once but not guarded

reasoning: Naive approach: delete-before-fetch so a crash can't double-send - rejected because then a crash loses the order entirely (under-charge / missing order, worse for the business). Alternative: server-side fingerprint of the body - rejected because legitimate re-orders of the same items would be wrongly deduped. Chosen path: client mints a UUID idempotency key when the order is enqueued and reuses it across all retries; the server upserts on that key inside a transaction so the charge is exactly-once. Cross-layer trace: UI enqueue writes {idemKey} into outbox -> SW claims rows then POSTs with Idempotency-Key header -> API checks idem table in same tx as charge -> returns the prior result on replay -> SW deletes only on 2xx -> tests simulate SW kill between POST and delete.

after_state.description: Each queued order carries a stable idempotency key; the SW claims rows before sending and deletes only after a confirmed 2xx; the server makes charge+insert exactly-once keyed on the idem key.

files.purpose: Claims then deletes-after-success with a stable idem key, so a crash mid-replay is safe to retry.
Mints one idem key per logical order so all retries share it across reloads/updates.
Exactly-once charge: idem table unique constraint + same key forwarded to the payment provider.
Durable cross-pod dedup store with a PK that makes concurrent inserts race-safe.

migration_strategy: Deploy idempotency table + API dedup first; old clients (no header) get a 400-soft fallback that logs and still charges once
Release new SW that mints idem keys; existing outbox rows backfilled with a generated key on next open
Canary: 5% of stores on new SW, watch duplicate-charge and dead-letter dashboards
Ramp to 100% over a week; add TTL sweep job for idempotency table at 14 days
Remove the soft-fallback once <0.1% of traffic lacks the header
Rollback: disable header requirement (soft mode) and revert SW; outbox rows remain valid
testing_strategy: Fixture: outbox with one pending order; mock fetch to resolve 200 then forcibly throw to emulate SW kill before delete. Assert (output) the POST carried the same Idempotency-Key on both attempts and (side-effect) db.orders has exactly one row and payments.charge called once. Negative/chaos test: fire two concurrent 'sync' events plus a 500 then 200 sequence; assert exactly one charge, outbox empties, and the idempotency unique constraint rejected the duplicate insert (caught and mapped to replay).
what_breaks_naive_approach: At the boundary between fetch-success and db.delete: if the SW is terminated there, the naive loop re-POSTs with no key and the server, having no dedup, charges a second time - the 480 daily double-charges.

title: Migrate a versionless IndexedDB store to a transactional upgrade pipeline without data loss
business_context: A healthcare intake PWA stores partially-completed patient forms locally for clinics with poor connectivity (3,400 tablets). A shipped schema change started reading a renamed field, and because the app opened IndexedDB with a bumped version but no upgrade migration, ~7% of in-progress forms became unreadable, blocking patient check-in and forcing paper fallback.
constraints: Migrations must be transactional: a failed upgrade must not corrupt the existing store
Tablets may skip versions (offline for weeks then jump v3->v7)
No data loss for in-progress forms (PHI; legally retained)
Upgrade must run inside the single versionchange transaction IndexedDB provides
Must support concurrent tabs without 'blocked' deadlock

before_state.description: openDB bumps the version but the onupgradeneeded handler only createsObjectStore on first install; field renames are not migrated, so newer code reads undefined and crashes on existing rows.

issues: No oldVersion-aware migration chain; v3->v7 jump runs nothing
Renamed field leaves legacy rows with undefined patient.fullName
No onblocked handler; second tab deadlocks the upgrade
Assumes new shape; legacy rows crash with TypeError reading 'fullName' of undefined

reasoning: Naive approach: keep bumping version and add a defensive try/catch in readForm - rejected because it hides data behind undefined and never actually migrates, so each new field rename re-breaks. Alternative: nuke and re-download from server on version mismatch - rejected because in-progress unsynced PHI forms only exist locally and would be permanently lost. Chosen path: an ordered migration registry keyed by target version, executed sequentially inside the single versionchange transaction using oldVersion. Cross-layer trace: open() reads e.oldVersion -> runs migrations (3]->[4..7) within the same tx -> each migration transforms rows via a cursor -> onblocked prompts other tabs to close -> readForm now sees normalized shape -> tests cover skipped-version jumps and a migration that throws mid-way (whole tx rolls back).

after_state.description: A registry of versioned migrations runs in order inside the versionchange transaction using the provided transaction; a thrown migration aborts the whole upgrade atomically, and onblocked is handled so multi-tab upgrades don't deadlock.

files.purpose: Declarative, ordered, idempotent migrations that transform existing rows via cursors.
Runs the full migration chain for any oldVersion atomically and handles multi-tab blocking.
Reads the normalized shape; tolerant of an empty name but never crashes.
Prevents the original bug: version is derived from the migration list, not hand-bumped.

migration_strategy: Land migration framework with v4 (rename backfill) as the first real migration; target derived from list
Ship to a 2% canary fleet; verify no 'upgrade aborted' telemetry and forms readable
Add onblocked UX: prompt user to close other tabs; verify no deadlocks in field
Ramp to 100% over 5 days; keep a 'paper fallback' flag as safety valve
Once stable, add v5-v7 cleanup migrations and remove legacy patientName reads
Rollback: a failed migration auto-aborts (tx.abort) leaving the old store intact at its prior version
testing_strategy: Use fake-indexeddb. Fixture: seed a v3 store with rows using legacy patientName. Open at target version and assert (output) readForm returns the name and (side-effect) rows now have patient.fullName and no patientName. Skipped-version test: seed v3, jump to v7, assert all four migrations ran in order. Chaos test: inject a migration whose up() throws at version 6; assert tx aborts, the database stays at version 5's data shape (no partial v6 index, no v7 mutation), and reopening retries cleanly.
what_breaks_naive_approach: At the onupgradeneeded boundary the naive handler runs no transform for an oldVersion jump, so existing rows keep the old shape while new code reads the new shape - every legacy row throws TypeError and the form is unreadable.

title: Build a delta-sync cursor with monotonic change tokens to replace full-table re-pulls on a logistics PWA
business_context: A last-mile delivery PWA (Vue 3) gives 9,000 drivers their stop list. On every reconnect the app re-downloads the entire route table (up to 4MB), burning data plans and taking 18s on rural LTE, during which drivers can't see new stops. The team wants incremental sync so reconnects transfer only changed stops.
constraints: Pull only rows changed since last sync; deletes must propagate too (tombstones)
Change token must be monotonic and resumable after partial download
Server has 40M stop rows across 200 routes; no full scans
Multiple drivers share a route; updates from dispatch must reach all within 30s
Backward compatible: old clients keep full-pull until upgraded

before_state.description: Client always GETs the full route; server returns everything; no notion of 'since'.

issues: Full re-download on every reconnect
db.clear drops locally-edited unsynced rows
No delete propagation except via clear (which also nukes pending edits)
No since-token filtering; always full scan-ish
Deleted stops simply vanish from the list, client never learns they were deleted

reasoning: Naive approach: filter by updatedAt > lastSync timestamp - rejected because wall-clock timestamps collide and skew causes missed or repeated rows across the 200-route write fan-out. Alternative: stream every change via WebSocket and never poll - rejected because drivers are offline for long stretches and would need a catch-up path anyway, so we'd build delta-sync regardless. Chosen path: a per-route monotonic sequence (changeSeq from a sequence/logical clock) returned as an opaque cursor; client sends cursor, server returns rows with seq>cursor plus tombstones, newest seq becomes next cursor. Cross-layer trace: UI reconnect -> pullDelta(cursor) -> API selects WHERE change_seq > cursor ORDER BY change_seq LIMIT N -> applies upserts+tombstones to IndexedDB without clearing -> stores max seq -> tests cover deletes and partial pages.

after_state.description: Server assigns a monotonic change_seq to every mutation; clients pull only rows above their cursor, including tombstones for deletes, and resume from the last applied seq.

files.purpose: Returns only changes above the cursor with stable ordering and pagination for resumability.
Database-assigned monotonic seq guarantees no collisions across concurrent dispatch writes.
Applies upserts+tombstones incrementally and persists the cursor per page so a dropped connection resumes.
Protects locally-edited unsynced rows from being clobbered by an incoming delta (replaces db.clear).

migration_strategy: Add change_seq columns+trigger+index; backfill seq for existing rows in batches
Deploy API supporting ?cursor= while keeping the legacy full-list endpoint
Ship client delta path behind flag delta_sync; default remains full-pull
Canary 3% of drivers; compare bytes-transferred and reconnect time vs control
Ramp to 100%; remove full-pull endpoint after 30 days of <1% legacy usage
Rollback: disable flag -> clients revert to full-pull; server endpoints both still live
testing_strategy: Fixture: seed 600 stops, set cursor=0, mutate 3 stops and delete 1 (deleted=true). Assert (output) pullDelta returns exactly the 4 changed rows over the right page boundary and (side-effect) IndexedDB has the 3 upserts, the tombstoned row removed, and cursor advanced to max seq. Negative/chaos: drop the connection after page 1 of 2; assert resume from stored cursor applies page 2 with no duplicate or skipped seq, and a locally-dirty row is NOT overwritten by an older-seq remote.
what_breaks_naive_approach: At reconnect the naive db.clear+full-put wipes locally-edited unsynced stops before the (possibly partial) re-download completes; a mid-download disconnect leaves the driver with an empty or partial route and lost edits.

title: Fix a stale-while-revalidate service worker that serves a poisoned HTML shell after deploys
business_context: A B2B analytics dashboard PWA (React) caches its app shell with stale-while-revalidate. After each deploy the new JS bundle hashes change but the cached index.html still references the old hashed chunks, so ~15% of returning users get a white screen ('ChunkLoadError') until a hard refresh, generating ~120 support tickets per release.
constraints: No white-screen on deploy; old shell must never reference missing chunks
Must keep offline support for the shell
Cannot force-logout users mid-session
Bundle hashes change every deploy; CDN immutable for hashed assets
Service worker update must not require users to close all tabs

before_state.description: SW caches index.html with stale-while-revalidate and serves the cached HTML which points at chunk hashes that the new deploy removed.

issues: Stale index.html references chunk URLs deleted by the new deploy -> ChunkLoadError
No cache versioning tied to build id
Hashed chunks fetched from network 404 with no recovery

files.content:
import('./App').then(({ App }) => render(App));
// dynamic import fails (ChunkLoadError) -> unhandled -> white screen
issues: No retry/reload on ChunkLoadError
No build-id check to detect shell/asset mismatch

reasoning: Naive approach: switch the shell to cache-first and bump SHELL name each deploy - rejected because cache-first serves an even more stale shell and the manual bump is error-prone. Alternative: network-first for navigations - rejected because it defeats offline support (no network = no shell). Chosen path: network-first-with-timeout for the navigation shell PLUS a build-id handshake so a stale shell that survives is detected and self-heals via one controlled reload; precache hashed assets at install keyed to build id. Cross-layer trace: deploy writes BUILD_ID into index.html and SW -> SW install precaches that build's chunks -> fetch serves fresh shell when online, falls back to last-good offline -> on ChunkLoadError the app compares build ids and triggers skipWaiting+reload once -> tests simulate deploy-with-removed-chunk.

after_state.description: Navigations are network-first with a short timeout falling back to the last fully-consistent cached shell; the SW precaches each build's hashed assets and a build-id mismatch triggers exactly one self-healing reload.

files.purpose: Network-first navigation with offline fallback; atomic per-build precache so the shell and its chunks always match.
Lets the app detect a stale shell vs current bundle to trigger a controlled heal.
Self-heals a single time on chunk mismatch and guards against reload loops via sessionStorage.
Single source of build id wired into shell, SW, and precache list so they can never disagree.

migration_strategy: Add build-id injection to CI; ship SW that precaches per-build assets behind a 5% rollout
Verify activate-time cleanup deletes prior shell caches without evicting in-flight clients
Roll to 50% across one release cycle; watch ChunkLoadError telemetry trend to ~0
Enable self-heal reload guard for all users after confirming no reload loops
Full rollout; remove legacy stale-while-revalidate shell handler
Rollback: revert SW to prior version; activate cleanup keeps only current cache so no poisoned state lingers
testing_strategy: Use a SW test harness (e.g. service-worker-mock). Fixture: install build A precaching chunkA.js; simulate deploy to build B where chunkA.js 404s. Assert (output) a navigation online returns build B's index.html and (side-effect) shell-A cache deleted on activate. Chaos test: go offline, assert navigation returns last-good shell B; then simulate a forced stale shell A surviving and assert exactly one reload fires (sessionStorage guard prevents a loop).
what_breaks_naive_approach: At the deploy boundary the stale-while-revalidate SW serves cached index.html that references chunk hashes the new deploy deleted from the CDN; the dynamic import 404s as ChunkLoadError and the app white-screens until a manual hard refresh.

title: Implement vector-clock conflict detection for offline inventory counts in a retail PWA
business_context: A retail stocktake PWA (Angular) lets staff count inventory on handhelds that are frequently offline in stockrooms. Two staff counting the same SKU on different devices currently produce a silent overwrite; the last device to sync wins, causing inventory discrepancies that triggered a $220k write-off last quarter when phantom stock was reordered.
constraints: Detect concurrent vs causal updates per SKU; surface true conflicts to a supervisor
Devices offline up to 3 days; no reliable clocks
Up to 6 devices may edit one SKU during a count
Server stateless across 8 nodes; no row locks during count windows
Conflict resolution must be auditable (who counted what, when)

before_state.description: Each count push sends qty+deviceTime; server stores whichever arrives last, masking concurrent disagreements.

issues: No causality tracking; concurrent counts silently overwrite
Date.now from unsynced handhelds is unreliable
No conflict surfaced to a human
Blind update, no version check
Cannot distinguish a stale overwrite from a legitimate update

reasoning: Naive approach: add an integer version and reject on mismatch (optimistic lock) - rejected because with 6 offline devices everyone but the first gets rejected and must redo work, and it still can't tell concurrent from stale. Alternative: full operational transform - rejected as overkill for scalar counts and hard to audit. Chosen path: a per-SKU version vector (deviceId -> counter); server compares incoming vector to stored: if it dominates, fast-forward; if concurrent, persist both into a conflicts table for supervisor resolution. Cross-layer trace: device increments its slot on each local count -> push sends {qty, vv} -> server compares vectors -> dominate=apply, concurrent=record conflict -> supervisor UI resolves -> resolution merges vectors -> tests cover concurrent and causal cases.

after_state.description: Each count carries a version vector; the server distinguishes causal updates (fast-forward) from true concurrency (recorded as an auditable conflict for supervisor resolution).

files.purpose: Pure version-vector ops to classify and merge causal history per SKU.
Increments this device's vector slot and reports server-detected conflicts to local UI.
Classifies each update; concurrency becomes an auditable conflict instead of a silent overwrite.
Durable, auditable conflict log feeding the supervisor resolution screen.

migration_strategy: Add vv column + conflicts table; default vv empty so existing rows behave like fast-forward
Deploy server vector logic; old clients (no vv) treated as a single legacy node slot
Ship new client behind flag vv_counts to a 2-store pilot during a real stocktake
Add supervisor resolution UI; ramp to all stores over one count cycle
Decommission blind PUT path once all devices send vectors
Rollback: flag off -> clients send plain qty mapped to legacy node; server still applies as fast-forward
testing_strategy: Fixture: SKU with stored vv {A:2}. Device B (offline) pushes qty=10 vv {A:2,B:1} -> assert (output) applied:true, fast-forward, and inventory.vv == {A:2,B:1}. Concurrency case: stored vv {A:1,B:1}, incoming {A:2,B:0} (concurrent) -> assert a conflicts row inserted (side-effect) and no qty overwrite. Chaos test: replay the same concurrent push twice; assert only one conflict row (idempotent on candidate set) and the inventory qty unchanged until a supervisor resolves.
what_breaks_naive_approach: At the server update boundary the blind PUT cannot tell a causal newer count from two genuinely concurrent counts, so it overwrites and the disagreement vanishes - producing phantom stock and the reorder write-off.

title: Add a durable offline mutation queue with exponential backoff and dead-letter to a FastAPI-backed PWA
business_context: A volunteer disaster-relief PWA (React + FastAPI) lets responders log resource needs from areas with intermittent connectivity. The current 'retry in a setInterval' loses queued mutations when the tab closes and hammers the API the instant signal returns, causing 503 storms when 2,000 responders reconnect at once after an outage.
constraints: Queue must survive tab close / app update (persisted, not in-memory)
Backoff with jitter to avoid thundering-herd on mass reconnect
Mutations must apply in submission order per entity
Auth tokens may expire while queued; refresh before replay
Max queue age 72h then dead-letter with user notification

before_state.description: An in-memory array retried on a fixed interval; closing the tab drops it and reconnect floods the API.

issues: In-memory: tab close loses the queue
No backoff -> reconnect storm of 2000 clients
Clears all on any pass even if some failed; ordering not guaranteed
Expired auth token never refreshed

reasoning: Naive approach: persist the array to localStorage and keep the interval - rejected because localStorage is synchronous, size-limited, and still floods on reconnect. Alternative: rely solely on Background Sync API - rejected because iOS Safari (a large share of responders) doesn't support it, so a JS-level durable queue is still required. Chosen path: an IndexedDB-backed queue processed by a single drainer with per-entity ordering, exponential backoff + jitter, token refresh on 401, and a 72h dead-letter. Cross-layer trace: UI enqueue -> IndexedDB add(seq) -> online event triggers drain -> drain processes in seq order, on 5xx schedules retry with backoff, on 401 refreshes token, on age>72h moves to dead-letter and notifies -> tests cover storm and token expiry.

after_state.description: A persisted IndexedDB queue drained by a single controller with ordered processing, jittered exponential backoff, token refresh, and dead-lettering.

files.purpose: Durable, ordered (autoIncrement seq) queue store plus a dead-letter store.
Single-flight ordered drainer with jittered backoff and dead-lettering to avoid storms and infinite retries.
Refreshes an expired token mid-replay so long-queued mutations don't permanently 401.
Persists mutations immediately and wires drain to the online event.

migration_strategy: Introduce IndexedDB queue alongside the old interval; new mutations route to it behind flag durable_queue
Migrate any in-memory pending into IndexedDB on first load after upgrade
Canary 5% of responders; verify reconnect storm flattens (p99 API 5xx drops)
Add dead-letter notification UI; ramp to 100% across one incident cycle
Remove the legacy setInterval path
Rollback: flag off -> revert to interval; queued IndexedDB items drained by a one-shot compatibility shim so nothing is stranded
testing_strategy: Use fake-indexeddb + mocked fetch. Fixture: enqueue 3 mutations for the same entity; assert (output) they POST in seq order and (side-effect) queue store empties after 200s. Token test: first call returns 401 then 200; assert refresh called once and the retried call carried the new token. Chaos/storm test: simulate 500 responses for N attempts and assert nextAt grows ~2^attempt with jitter and only ONE drain runs concurrently (single-flight guard), preventing a flood; finally assert age>72h items land in 'dead' and notifyUser fired.
what_breaks_naive_approach: At reconnect the naive interval fires immediately for every client with no backoff and re-sends the whole array, so 2,000 simultaneous reconnects produce a synchronized 503 storm; and tab-close before a successful pass loses the queue entirely.

title: Replace polling reconciliation with a two-phase pull/push sync engine for a Postgres-backed Nuxt PWA
business_context: A B2B field-sales PWA (Nuxt 3) lets reps manage opportunities offline. The current sync polls every 30s and applies server rows blindly over local edits, so a rep's unsynced note vanishes when the poll lands first. ~200 reps report lost edits weekly; deals stall because notes disappear.
constraints: Local unsynced edits must never be overwritten by a pull
Push and pull must be ordered: push local changes, then pull remote, then reconcile
Postgres is source of truth; 1.2M opportunities
Auth via short-lived JWT; sync runs in a Web Worker
No duplicate rows after a push that the pull then echoes back

before_state.description: A timer pulls server rows and overwrites IndexedDB unconditionally; local dirty edits are lost when the poll wins the race.

issues: Blind put overwrites locally-dirty rows
No push of local changes; pull-only
since=Date.now() loses changes during the request window

reasoning: Naive approach: skip put when local row is newer by updatedAt - rejected because clocks differ and a dirty local edit older than a server change still must not be silently dropped without merge. Alternative: server-authoritative with no local edits (online-only forms) - rejected because the whole value prop is offline editing. Chosen path: a two-phase engine - push all dirty rows first (server returns canonical), then pull deltas, reconciling per row by base-version so echoed-back pushes don't duplicate and concurrent server edits are surfaced. Cross-layer trace: worker tick -> collect dirty -> push (server assigns rev) -> clear dirty for accepted -> pull since cursor -> for each remote, if local dirty keep+flag conflict else apply -> persist cursor -> tests cover push-echo and dirty-protection.

after_state.description: Sync runs push-then-pull; dirty rows are pushed and reconciled by base rev, pulls never clobber unsynced edits, and echoed pushes are deduped by rev.

files.purpose: Ordered push-then-pull with dirty protection and rev-based dedup of echoed writes.
Optimistic concurrency by base rev; mismatched base returns 409 for client-side reconciliation.
Runs the engine off the main thread with adaptive interval; failures preserve the cursor.
Adds the dirty index and baseRev metadata needed for push/pull reconciliation.

migration_strategy: Add rev/base columns + delta endpoint server-side; backfill rev=1
Add dirty index via IndexedDB migration; existing rows dirty=0
Ship engine behind flag two_phase_sync to 5% of reps
Compare lost-edit reports and duplicate-row counts vs control; ramp to 100%
Remove the legacy poll loop after 30 days
Rollback: flag off reverts to poll; dirty rows remain in IndexedDB and re-push when re-enabled
testing_strategy: Fixture: local opp with dirty=1 and a concurrent server delta for the same id. Assert (output) pull does NOT overwrite the dirty row and (side-effect) the row keeps its local patch. Push-echo test: push a dirty row, server returns rev N, then the delta echoes the same row at rev N; assert no duplicate and dirty cleared. Chaos test: push returns 409; assert conflict object stored and the local edit preserved (not silently dropped); cursor unchanged so retry is safe.
what_breaks_naive_approach: At the 30s poll boundary the blind put lands while a local edit is still dirty/unsynced and overwrites it; the rep's note is gone with no trace.

title: Coordinate multi-tab sync with a Web Locks leader election to stop redundant network and races
business_context: A project-management PWA (React) is often open in 4-6 tabs per user. Each tab independently runs the sync loop, so the same deltas are pulled 5x (wasting ~3x backend read capacity) and two tabs occasionally push the same dirty row, creating duplicate comments. ~5% of users see duplicate comments.
constraints: Exactly one tab performs sync at a time (leader)
Leader must fail over within 2s if the tab closes
Non-leader tabs must still see updates (broadcast results)
Works without SharedWorker (Safari quirks)
No duplicate pushes across tabs even during failover

before_state.description: Every tab runs its own setInterval sync; no coordination, so pulls duplicate and pushes race.

files.content: setInterval(() => runSync(), 10000); // every tab does this independently
issues: N tabs => N× pulls of identical deltas
Two tabs can push the same dirty row -> duplicate server writes
No leader, no shared cursor

reasoning: Naive approach: use localStorage 'lock' key with timestamps - rejected because it's racy (no atomic CAS) and stale locks deadlock after a crash. Alternative: SharedWorker as the single sync owner - rejected because Safari's SharedWorker support is unreliable for this user base. Chosen path: the Web Locks API (navigator.locks) for leader election with an exclusive held lock; the holder syncs and broadcasts results via BroadcastChannel; lock auto-releases on tab close giving sub-second failover. Cross-layer trace: each tab requests the 'sync-leader' lock -> the granted tab loops sync -> posts applied deltas on BroadcastChannel -> other tabs apply to their in-memory cache + IndexedDB read -> on leader close the lock frees and a waiting tab is granted -> tests simulate leader crash mid-push.

after_state.description: One tab holds an exclusive Web Lock and is the sole syncer; it broadcasts results to peers; lock release on close yields automatic failover with a stored cursor preventing duplicate pushes.

files.purpose: Web Locks guarantees a single leader; BroadcastChannel fans results out to non-leaders.
Atomic dirty-claim in IndexedDB so even mid-failover no two leaders push the same row.
Recovers rows abandoned by a crashed leader without double-applying (server idempotency).
Lets non-leader tabs reflect updates instantly from broadcasts without their own network calls.

migration_strategy: Ship leader election behind flag single_leader_sync; feature-detect navigator.locks (fallback to old loop)
Canary 10% of users; measure backend read QPS drop and duplicate-comment rate
Add BroadcastChannel propagation; verify non-leader tabs update within 1s
Ramp to 100% for browsers supporting Web Locks; legacy browsers keep per-tab loop
Remove per-tab loop for supported browsers after 30 days
Rollback: flag off -> per-tab sync; dirty claims expire via reclaimStale so nothing strands
testing_strategy: Fixture: simulate two tab contexts sharing a fake-indexeddb; both call startSync. Assert (output) only one acquires the lock and runs runSync, and (side-effect) the API pull is called once per cycle not twice. Failover test: kill the leader (resolve its lock promise) mid-push with a row marked inflight; assert a second tab acquires the lock, reclaimStale requeues the row, and the server's idempotency key prevents a duplicate comment. Chaos: rapid open/close of 6 tabs; assert no duplicate pushes via server write-count assertion.
what_breaks_naive_approach: With no coordination two tabs read the same dirty row and POST it concurrently; the server, lacking cross-tab dedup, writes two comments - the 5% duplicate rate - and N tabs multiply backend read load.

title: Introduce a Yjs CRDT document with IndexedDB + WebSocket providers for collaborative offline doc editing
business_context: A knowledge-base PWA (Next.js) lets teams co-author runbooks. The current autosave POSTs the whole markdown body and last-write-wins, so two editors (one offline) routinely clobber each other; the on-call team lost a 4,000-word incident runbook during a real incident. They need true collaborative + offline-capable editing.
constraints: Concurrent edits from online and offline users must merge character-level
Offline edits up to 8h must merge cleanly on reconnect
Document up to 200KB; sync must be incremental (op-based)
Server relay is stateless; persistence in Postgres as a Yjs update log
Awareness (cursors) must not be persisted, only doc state

before_state.description: Autosave PUTs the entire body; offline editor's reconnect overwrites whatever changed server-side.

issues: Whole-document overwrite loses concurrent edits
Offline editor's stale body clobbers server on reconnect
No merge, no character-level resolution

reasoning: Naive approach: diff-patch the markdown server-side with a 3-way merge - rejected because text 3-way merges produce conflict markers users can't resolve mid-incident and still fail on concurrent same-line edits. Alternative: operational transform (e.g. ShareDB) - rejected because OT needs a central authoritative server to transform ops, breaking the offline-first requirement. Chosen path: Yjs CRDT with y-indexeddb (offline persistence) + y-websocket (relay), persisting the update log to Postgres; merges are automatic and commutative. Cross-layer trace: editor binds to Y.Text -> local edits update the doc + persist to IndexedDB -> websocket provider syncs updates online -> server relays + appends updates to Postgres log -> offline edits replay and merge on reconnect -> tests assert concurrent inserts both survive.

after_state.description: The document is a Yjs doc persisted offline via y-indexeddb and synced via y-websocket; the server relays and durably logs updates, giving automatic character-level merges online and offline.

files.purpose: Wires offline persistence + relay sync into one Yjs doc; merges are automatic.
Hydrates instantly from offline state then live-merges remote updates without a manual save.
Stateless relay that hydrates a room from the Postgres update log and appends new updates.
Durable, append-only Yjs update log in Postgres (incremental, not whole-doc).

migration_strategy: Stand up y-websocket relay + ydoc_updates table; no client change yet
One-time import: convert each doc's current markdown into an initial Yjs update and seed the log
Ship Yjs editor behind flag crdt_editor to an internal team
Run dual-write (old PUT + Yjs) during canary to keep a fallback snapshot; ramp to 100%
Add update-log compaction job; remove the old whole-doc PUT path
Rollback: flag off reverts to markdown editor; the last compacted snapshot exports back to plain markdown
testing_strategy: Fixture: two Y.Doc instances (online A, offline B) editing the same paragraph; apply A's insert at pos 5 and B's insert at pos 5 while disconnected. Reconnect (applyUpdate both ways) and assert (output) both inserts present, deterministic order, no data loss. Side-effect: assert ydoc_updates has the appended updates and a snapshot compaction reduces row count without changing doc content. Chaos test: replay the same update twice and drop one mid-stream; assert final doc state identical (idempotent applyUpdate) and IndexedDB-hydrated offline open matches server state.
what_breaks_naive_approach: At reconnect the offline editor's whole-body PUT overwrites every server-side change made while it was offline; concurrent edits on the same lines are lost - exactly how the incident runbook was destroyed.

title: Precache and range-serve large audio files so an offline podcast PWA supports seek and resume
business_context: A language-learning PWA (Svelte) lets users download lessons (20-60MB audio) for offline study on commutes. The current SW caches the whole response but breaks <audio> seeking offline because it can't satisfy HTTP Range requests, so users can't scrub or resume mid-lesson; 30% abandon a lesson they can't seek in.
constraints: Must satisfy 206 Partial Content / Range requests from cache when offline
Downloads up to 60MB; respect a 500MB per-origin storage budget with eviction
Resume a partially-downloaded lesson, not restart
Background download must survive tab navigation
Works on iOS Safari (no Background Fetch) and Chrome (with it)

before_state.description: SW caches the full 200 response; offline seeks send Range requests that the cache match returns as 200 full bodies, which the media element rejects for seeking.

issues: Range requests not honored; <audio> can't seek offline
Whole-file cached with no storage budget or eviction
No resume of partial downloads

reasoning: Naive approach: store the file and on a Range request return the full 200 body - rejected because Safari's media element requires a 206 with correct Content-Range to seek and will error otherwise. Alternative: store the audio in IndexedDB as a Blob and reconstruct ranges in JS - rejected as workable but adds a slicing layer; we still need to intercept Range. Chosen path: cache the full body in the Cache API but, in the SW fetch handler, parse the Range header and synthesize a 206 with the correct slice and Content-Range; add a quota-aware downloader with resume. Cross-layer trace: UI 'download' -> downloader fetches with resume (Range from byte offset) storing into Cache -> SW fetch parses Range -> slices cached arrayBuffer -> returns 206 -> media seeks offline -> eviction job enforces 500MB -> tests assert 206 + Content-Range.

after_state.description: The SW synthesizes 206 Partial Content responses from the cached body so offline seeking works; a quota-aware downloader supports resume and LRU eviction.

files.purpose: Honors Range by slicing the cached buffer and returning a correct 206 so offline seek works.
Resumable download using Range from the last byte offset, persisting progress for restart-safety.
LRU eviction keeps the origin under the 500MB budget so downloads don't fail with QuotaExceeded.
Uses Background Fetch on Chrome and a foreground resumable fallback on iOS Safari.

migration_strategy: Ship the 206-synthesis SW handler behind flag range_audio for new downloads only
Migrate existing cached files lazily (they already have full bodies; Range works immediately)
Canary 10%; verify offline seek works on iOS Safari and Chrome via real-device tests
Add quota eviction + resume; ramp to 100%
Enable Background Fetch path where supported; keep foreground fallback
Rollback: flag off reverts to whole-200 serving (loses seek but plays); cached files untouched
testing_strategy: Fixture: cache a 10MB synthetic audio buffer. Send a request with Range: bytes=1048576-2097151; assert (output) status 206, correct Content-Range and Content-Length, and the returned bytes equal buf.slice(...). Resume test: simulate a download interrupted at 4MB; restart and assert it issues Range: bytes=4194304- and final cached length equals total (side-effect: meta.complete true). Chaos test: fill storage past 500MB and assert ensureBudget evicts LRU entries (cache.delete called) until under 80% budget, and an in-progress download is not evicted.
what_breaks_naive_approach: At an offline seek the media element issues a Range request; the naive cache.match returns a 200 full body with no Content-Range, so Safari's audio element rejects it and seeking/resume fails entirely.

title: Add causal ordering and tombstone GC to a Mongo change-stream sync for a social PWA feed
business_context: A community social PWA (React Native Web) syncs a user's feed and their offline likes/comments. Deletes are implemented by removing rows, so an offline client that liked a now-deleted post resurrects it on sync (a 'zombie post' bug), and the tombstone-less design also lets edits arrive before the create they depend on, showing comments on missing posts. ~2% of feeds show zombies or orphans.
constraints: Deletes must propagate as tombstones, not row removal
Operations have causal dependencies (comment depends on post create)
MongoDB change streams drive server fanout; client buffers offline ops
Tombstones must be garbage-collected after all clients have seen them
Offline window up to 5 days; up to 50k ops buffered per client

before_state.description: Deletes remove documents; sync ships raw inserts/updates with no dependency info, so out-of-order delivery resurrects deleted posts and shows orphaned comments.

issues: Delete removes the row, so a later offline op resurrects it (zombie)
No causal deps: a comment op can apply before its post create (orphan)
No ordering guarantee from the change stream buffer

reasoning: Naive approach: sort ops by server timestamp before applying - rejected because timestamps don't encode causality (a like generated offline has an old ts but depends on a post that may have been deleted). Alternative: require the client to refetch the full feed when any conflict is detected - rejected because it defeats offline-first and is expensive at scale. Chosen path: tombstones with a per-entity Lamport/version stamp plus explicit dependency refs; apply buffers ops until deps are satisfied and treats tombstones as terminal so resurrection is impossible; GC tombstones once a low-watermark (min acked seq across clients) passes them. Cross-layer trace: client op records deps -> server stamps seq via change stream order -> apply checks tombstone+deps -> defers unsatisfied ops -> tombstone blocks zombie -> GC sweeps tombstones below watermark -> tests cover zombie and orphan.

after_state.description: Deletes are tombstones with a terminal flag; ops carry dependency refs and apply only when deps exist; tombstones are GC'd below a global low-watermark.

files.purpose: Tombstones block resurrection and dependency checks prevent orphans; unsatisfied ops are deferred.
Stamps every change with a monotonic seq and records deletes as ops, not row removals.
Garbage-collects tombstones only once every client has advanced past them, bounding growth.
Re-attempts deferred ops once their dependencies (e.g. the post create) arrive.

migration_strategy: Add ops collection + seq counter + tombstone field; deletes start dual-writing tombstones
Backfill: convert recent hard-deletes into tombstones from the oplog where available
Ship client apply with tombstone/dep logic behind flag causal_sync to 3%
Verify zombie/orphan rate drops; add client_acks reporting for GC watermark
Ramp to 100%; enable tombstone GC job once acks are reported fleet-wide
Rollback: flag off -> client ignores deps/tombstones gracefully (tombstones render as hidden); server still records seq
testing_strategy: Fixture: ops stream where post P is created (seq1), deleted as tombstone (seq3), and an offline 'like' on P arrives with seq2<3. Assert (output) P stays deleted (no zombie) and (side-effect) posts store has P as tombstone, not a live row. Orphan test: deliver a comment op (deps:[postX]) before postX's create; assert it is deferred, then arrives after postX create on flush. Chaos/GC test: set client_acks below the tombstone seq and assert GC does NOT delete it; advance all acks past it and assert GC removes it exactly once.
what_breaks_naive_approach: At apply time the naive code removes the row on delete, so a later out-of-order offline op simply re-inserts the post (zombie); and with no dependency gate a comment renders before its post exists (orphan).

title: Fix a service worker that caches authenticated API responses across users on shared kiosks
business_context: A clinic check-in PWA runs on shared waiting-room kiosks (one device, many patients). The SW caches GET /api/me and patient records; after patient A leaves, patient B's kiosk serves patient A's cached PHI from the SW cache - a HIPAA incident reported twice in one month.
constraints: Never serve one user's authenticated response to another user
Logout must purge all per-user cached data immediately
Public assets must stay cached for offline shell
Token rotation on each patient session
No PHI persisted to disk beyond the active session

before_state.description: The SW caches all GET responses including authenticated PHI with a shared cache key (URL only), so a new patient sees the prior patient's cached data.

issues: Cache key is URL-only; /api/me returns the previous patient's data
Authenticated PHI written to a persistent cache
No purge on logout/session change

reasoning: Naive approach: add the bearer token to the cache key (Vary on Authorization) - rejected because it still persists PHI to disk and a token rotation leaves stale PHI cached and evictable by anyone with devtools. Alternative: disable the SW entirely on the kiosk - rejected because it kills offline shell support the clinic needs during network blips. Chosen path: never cache authenticated/PHI responses (network-only with a no-store policy), cache only public shell assets, and on session end broadcast a purge that clears any per-user caches; keep ephemeral data in memory only. Cross-layer trace: fetch handler classifies request (public vs authed) -> authed = network-only -> login/logout posts a message -> SW purges 'api' cache + memory -> shell stays cached -> tests assert no cross-user hit and purge on logout.

after_state.description: Authenticated/PHI responses are network-only and never persisted; only public assets are cached; session end triggers an immediate cache purge.

files.purpose: Public-only caching with network-only PHI and a message-driven purge so no patient data persists.
Logout immediately purges SW caches and in-memory PHI and rotates the token.
Forces no-store on authed reads and ends the session on 401 to prevent stale-token reuse.
On upgrade, deletes the legacy authed cache so prior PHI cannot leak after deploy.

migration_strategy: Ship SW update that, on activate, deletes the legacy 'api' cache (immediate remediation)
Add network-only PHI classification and public-only caching behind no flag (security fix, fast-track)
Wire endSession purge into the existing logout/idle-timeout flow
Verify on kiosks via QA: patient A logout then patient B sees no A data
Add an idle-timeout auto-endSession (e.g. 90s) as defense-in-depth
Rollback: only the prior SW is restorable, but activate-purge still runs, so no PHI lingers either way
testing_strategy: Fixture: SW test harness; request /api/me as token A (cached attempt). Assert (output) the response is NOT served from cache on a second request and (side-effect) the 'api' cache has zero entries for /api/me. Cross-user test: simulate token A then endSession then token B; assert B's /api/me hits the network and never returns A's body. Chaos test: kill network during an authed GET and assert a 503 offline response (not a stale cached PHI hit), and assert activate-time purge deletes a pre-seeded legacy 'api' cache.
what_breaks_naive_approach: At the cache key boundary the URL-only key for /api/me returns the previous patient's PHI to the next patient on the same kiosk - a HIPAA cross-user data leak.

title: Build a sync checkpoint + Merkle digest to detect and repair silent divergence in a gaming PWA
business_context: A turn-based strategy game PWA caches the player's match state and progression offline. Subtle bugs (dropped ops, partial applies) leave a small fraction of clients silently diverged from the server, causing 'impossible' game states and ~1,500 weekly support tickets ('my units disappeared'). They need a way to detect divergence cheaply and self-repair.
constraints: Detect divergence without re-downloading full state (states up to 2MB)
Repair must be targeted: only resync diverged subtrees
Cheating-resistant: server state is authoritative
Checkpoint comparison must be O(log n) network
Works across 300k active clients without overloading the API

before_state.description: No integrity check; client assumes its local state matches the server. Divergence is invisible until a player notices and files a ticket.

issues: No verification that local state equals server after applying ops
A single dropped op diverges forever, invisibly
Repair requires a full re-download (expensive at 2MB x 300k)

reasoning: Naive approach: periodically re-download the full state and compare - rejected because 2MB x 300k clients is a bandwidth bomb and most clients are fine. Alternative: a single whole-state hash - rejected because a mismatch tells you something diverged but not what, forcing a full resync anyway. Chosen path: a Merkle tree over state partitions (e.g. by region/inventory bucket); client and server exchange the root, then descend only mismatched branches to fetch the minimal diverged leaves. Cross-layer trace: after applying ops the client computes its Merkle root -> sends root at checkpoint -> server compares -> if mismatch, client walks down comparing child hashes -> fetches only diverged leaf partitions -> reapplies authoritative leaves -> tests inject a dropped op and assert targeted repair.

after_state.description: A Merkle digest over state partitions lets client and server compare roots in O(1) and repair only the diverged leaves in O(log n) network.

files.purpose: Builds a Merkle tree over partitions and computes the minimal set of diverged leaf keys.
Compares roots first (cheap) and repairs only diverged partitions when they differ.
Server exposes its root and authoritative leaf partitions for targeted repair.
Adds a periodic Merkle checkpoint after op application to catch and self-heal divergence.

migration_strategy: Add server merkle-root/tree/leaves endpoints with a cached, write-invalidated tree
Ship client checkpoint behind flag merkle_check at a low frequency (every 50 syncs) to 1%
Measure divergence-detected rate and repair bandwidth; tune partitioning granularity
Increase frequency and ramp to 100%; add a dashboard for diverged-leaf counts
Use detection data to fix the underlying op-apply bug, then keep checkpoint as a safety net
Rollback: flag off disables checkpoints; op sync continues unaffected
testing_strategy: Fixture: identical client/server partition maps; assert (output) checkpoint returns ok with no leaf fetch (side-effect: /leaves not called). Divergence test: drop one op so a single partition diverges; assert diffLeaves returns exactly that one key, /leaves is requested for only that key, and after repair the local root equals the server root. Chaos test: corrupt 3 random partitions and a clock-skewed checkpoint; assert exactly those 3 leaves are fetched (O(log n), not full state) and the repaired state hashes equal.
what_breaks_naive_approach: At op-apply a single dropped or partial op silently diverges local state from server forever; with no integrity check the only signal is a player ticket, and the only fix is a full 2MB re-download.

title: Convert blocking IndexedDB writes on the main thread to a batched Web Worker pipeline for a 60fps PWA
business_context: A drawing/whiteboard PWA (React) persists every stroke point to IndexedDB on the main thread. During fast drawing this fires thousands of tiny put() calls, blocking the main thread and dropping the canvas to ~20fps; users report 'laggy ink'. The app also loses the last few strokes on crash because writes are unbatched and unflushed.
constraints: Maintain 60fps during continuous drawing
No stroke loss on crash/refresh (durable within 500ms)
Writes must be ordered per board
Worker must coalesce bursts but bound latency to 250ms
Backward compatible with existing on-disk stroke format

before_state.description: Each pointermove writes one stroke point to IndexedDB synchronously on the main thread, saturating it and dropping frames; unbatched writes also leave a gap on crash.

issues: One IndexedDB transaction per pointer event blocks the main thread
Thousands of tiny txns/sec drop the canvas below 60fps
No batching/flush guarantee; crash loses recent points

reasoning: Naive approach: debounce writes by 1s on the main thread - rejected because it still runs on the main thread and a 1s window loses up to a second of ink on crash. Alternative: write to localStorage synchronously - rejected because localStorage is synchronous and even slower, blocking worse. Chosen path: post points to a dedicated Web Worker over a transferable buffer; the worker coalesces into batched putAll transactions every 250ms (or on visibilitychange) keeping the main thread free and bounding durability latency. Cross-layer trace: pointermove pushes into a ring buffer -> rAF posts the buffer to the worker -> worker batches into one IDB tx per board per flush -> flush on hidden/beforeunload guarantees durability -> tests assert fps-safe batching and crash-safety.

after_state.description: Points stream to a Web Worker via postMessage; the worker batches them into ordered putAll transactions every 250ms and on page-hide, keeping the main thread at 60fps and bounding data loss to one flush window.

files.purpose: Streams points off the main thread via transferable buffers and forces a flush on page-hide.
Coalesces bursts into a single ordered transaction per 250ms or on demand, durable and off-thread.
Reads strokes in stable order, tolerating legacy rows without a seq for backward compat.
Non-destructive schema bump so existing on-disk strokes remain readable.

migration_strategy: Introduce the worker pipeline behind flag worker_persist; main-thread path stays default
Verify frame timing on mid-tier devices stays <16ms during stress drawing
Canary 10%; compare dropped-frame and lost-stroke metrics
Add visibilitychange/beforeunload flush; ramp to 100%
Remove main-thread per-point write path
Rollback: flag off reverts to main-thread writes; on-disk format unchanged so no data migration needed
testing_strategy: Fixture: feed 5,000 synthetic points in a burst to the worker. Assert (output) they persist in seq order and (side-effect) the number of IndexedDB transactions is ~ (duration/250ms), not 5,000 (assert via tx spy). Performance assertion: main-thread long-tasks stay under 50ms during the burst. Chaos test: post points then immediately dispatch 'flush-now' (simulating page hide) and assert all buffered points are durably written before the worker idles; drop a postMessage and assert no out-of-order seq.
what_breaks_naive_approach: At each pointermove the synchronous main-thread IndexedDB transaction blocks rendering; thousands per second saturate the main thread, dropping the canvas to ~20fps, and the unbatched, unflushed writes lose the last strokes on crash.

title: Add server-driven schema-version negotiation so old offline clients sync without breaking
business_context: A construction-management PWA (Ember) has clients that go offline for weeks. A breaking change to the sync payload (renamed + restructured fields) broke every client that hadn't refreshed, returning 422s on push and silently dropping their queued offline work; ~600 superintendents lost a day's logs. The team needs forward/backward-compatible sync.
constraints: Old clients (up to 3 app versions behind) must keep syncing
Server must understand multiple payload schema versions simultaneously
Newer fields must degrade gracefully on old clients
No data loss for queued offline mutations during the transition
Schema version negotiated per-request, not per-deploy

before_state.description: The push endpoint assumes the latest payload schema; older clients send the old shape and get 422, dropping their queued mutations.

issues: Single hard-coded schema; old clients 422 and their queue is dropped
No version negotiation or upcasting
Breaking field rename has no compatibility layer

reasoning: Naive approach: keep the latest schema and tell users to update the app - rejected because offline clients literally cannot update until online and would lose queued work in the meantime. Alternative: maintain N parallel endpoints (/v3/sync, /v4/sync) - rejected because the matrix explodes and clients don't always know their version vs server's. Chosen path: a version header plus server-side upcaster chain that transforms any supported old payload to the canonical internal shape, and a downcaster for responses; version negotiated per request. Cross-layer trace: client sends X-Schema-Version -> server picks the upcaster path schemaN->canonical -> validates canonical -> persists -> downcasts response to the client's version -> tests cover a v3 client against a v5 server.

after_state.description: Each request declares its schema version; the server upcasts old payloads through a transform chain to a canonical model and downcasts responses, so clients 3 versions behind still sync without loss.

files.purpose: Negotiates schema per request and routes through up/downcasting so old clients still succeed.
Pure, composable transform chains making any supported version interoperable with canonical.
Sends the client's schema version and crucially retains the queue on 426 instead of dropping it.
Validates only the canonical internal shape, so validation logic stays single and stable.

migration_strategy: Introduce canonical model + registry with upcasters for currently-live client versions (v3,v4)
Deploy server accepting X-Schema-Version while still tolerating header-less (assume oldest supported)
Ship clients that send their version and retain queue on 426
Run with all live versions for a release cycle; monitor 422/426 rates
Add new schema versions only by appending an upcaster, never breaking old ones
Rollback: registry is additive; reverting a server deploy keeps all upcasters, so no client breaks
testing_strategy: Fixture: a v3 payload (lat/lng, assignee as string) posted with X-Schema-Version:3 to a CANONICAL=5 server. Assert (output) 200 and the persisted row is canonical {location:{lat,lng}, assignee:{id}} (side-effect: db.logs row shape) and the response downcasts back to v3 shape. Negative test: an unupcastable payload returns 422 with detail and does NOT drop the client queue. Chaos test: a too-old v1 client gets 426; assert the client retains its outbox (no delete) and shows the upgrade banner, then succeeds after updating to v4.
what_breaks_naive_approach: At the push boundary the single hard-coded schema 422s any client that hasn't refreshed; the naive client treats 422 as terminal and drops its outbox, losing a day of offline logs for 600 users.

title: Implement encrypted offline storage with per-session key wrapping for a fintech PWA
business_context: A personal-finance PWA (React) caches account balances and transactions in IndexedDB for offline viewing. A pen-test found that anyone with device access can read plaintext financial data via DevTools. Compliance now requires offline data to be encrypted at rest with keys never persisted in plaintext, without breaking offline access for the legitimate logged-in user.
constraints: Cached data encrypted at rest (AES-GCM) with keys derived from user auth
Key material never stored in plaintext in IndexedDB/localStorage
Offline access must still work after a refresh (key re-derivable from a wrapped key)
Logout must make cached data cryptographically inaccessible
No measurable UI lag decrypting a 5,000-transaction list

before_state.description: Transactions are written to IndexedDB as plaintext JSON; any device-local actor can read them.

issues: Plaintext financial data readable via DevTools
No key management; nothing encrypted
Logout doesn't render cached data inaccessible

reasoning: Naive approach: encrypt with a key hard-coded in the JS bundle - rejected because the key ships to every client and is trivially extractable, providing no real protection. Alternative: store the raw key in IndexedDB so it survives refresh - rejected because that just moves the plaintext secret onto disk. Chosen path: derive a master key from the user's session (PBKDF2 over a server-issued secret) as a non-extractable CryptoKey, wrap a per-DB data key with it, store only the wrapped key; decrypt records on read; logout drops the unwrapped key. Cross-layer trace: login obtains a key-derivation secret -> derive non-extractable KEK via WebCrypto -> generate/wrap data key, persist wrapped only -> writes encrypt with data key (AES-GCM, per-record IV) -> reads decrypt -> logout zeroizes in-memory keys -> tests assert ciphertext on disk and logout inaccessibility.

after_state.description: Records are AES-GCM encrypted with a data key wrapped by a session-derived non-extractable KEK; only wrapped key material is persisted, and logout makes the data unreadable.

files.purpose: Derives a non-extractable KEK from the session secret and persists only the wrapped data key.
Encrypts each record with a per-record IV before persisting and decrypts on read.
AES-GCM authenticated encryption with fresh IVs and integrity (tamper detection on read).
Establishes keys on login and drops the in-memory data key on logout to lock the cache.

migration_strategy: Ship crypto layer; on first login, encrypt existing plaintext rows in place then mark migrated
Read path supports both ciphertext and (briefly) legacy plaintext during migration window
Canary 5%; verify decrypt latency on a 5,000-row list under 50ms (Web Crypto is native)
Ramp to 100%; remove the legacy plaintext read fallback
Add key-rotation support (rewrap data key on credential change)
Rollback: keep wrapped key; if disabled, app falls back to network-only (no plaintext ever re-written)
testing_strategy: Fixture: cache 100 txns then inspect the raw IndexedDB rows; assert (output/side-effect) each row has {iv, ct} and contains no plaintext substring of any amount or merchant. Round-trip: readTxns returns the original objects. Logout test: call onLogout then readTxns and assert it cannot decrypt (data key null -> error), i.e. data is inaccessible. Tamper/chaos test: flip a byte in a stored ct and assert decryptRecord throws (GCM auth tag failure) rather than returning corrupted data.
what_breaks_naive_approach: At rest the plaintext IndexedDB rows are readable by anyone with device/DevTools access; there is no key boundary at all, so financial PII is fully exposed - the pen-test finding.

title: Replace navigator.onLine guard with reachability probing and event-driven sync triggering
business_context: A warehouse-scanning PWA (Vue) gates sync on navigator.onLine. Warehouse Wi-Fi shows 'online' while the captive portal or backend is unreachable, so scans queue but never flush until a manual reload; pickers don't notice for hours, delaying ~300 shipments/day. The team needs reliable reachability detection and automatic flush on real connectivity.
constraints: navigator.onLine is unreliable behind captive portals; need real reachability
Probe must be cheap and not hammer the API (backoff when down)
Sync must trigger automatically when reachability returns
Avoid false 'online' from cached probe responses
Detect backend-down (5xx) distinctly from network-down

before_state.description: Sync only runs when navigator.onLine is true; behind a captive portal that flag is true but the API is unreachable, so the queue never flushes.

issues: navigator.onLine reports true behind captive portals
No actual reachability check to the backend
No retry/backoff; a single failure leaves the queue stuck until reload

reasoning: Naive approach: just retry flush on a timer regardless of onLine - rejected because it hammers the API while genuinely offline and still can't distinguish backend-down from net-down. Alternative: rely on the Network Information API - rejected because it doesn't tell you whether YOUR backend is reachable (captive portal still passes). Chosen path: an active reachability probe to a cache-busting health endpoint with exponential backoff, emitting a 'reachable' event that drives sync; classify net-error vs 5xx for UX. Cross-layer trace: online/visibility events kick a probe -> probe fetches /health?nonce with no-store -> on success emit reachable -> flush runs -> on failure schedule backoff probe -> UI shows distinct net-down vs backend-down -> tests cover captive portal.

after_state.description: An active reachability monitor probes a no-store health endpoint with backoff and emits a reachable event that triggers flush; it distinguishes network-down from backend-5xx.

files.purpose: Actively verifies backend reachability with cache-busting + timeout and backoff, emitting distinct states.
Flushes only on confirmed reachability and stops cleanly on error to retry on the next probe.
Shows pickers a truthful, distinct status so a captive-portal stall is visible, not silent.
A no-store health endpoint that reflects real backend readiness (DB ping) for honest probes.

migration_strategy: Add /health endpoint with no-store + DB ping
Ship reachability monitor behind flag reachability_probe; sync still also listens to online
Canary in one warehouse; verify queue flushes within one probe interval of real connectivity
Replace navigator.onLine gate with reachability state; ramp to all warehouses
Tune probe interval/backoff from telemetry; remove legacy onLine gate
Rollback: flag off reverts to navigator.onLine; queue persists so nothing is lost
testing_strategy: Fixture: mock fetch to /health returning network error (captive portal) while navigator.onLine=true; assert (output) state emits 'net-down' and (side-effect) flush does NOT run. Recovery test: switch /health to 200 and assert a 'reachable' emit triggers queue flush and removal of sent ops. Backend-down test: /health 503; assert state 'backend-down' (distinct) and backoff grows. Chaos: ensure probe uses a unique nonce so a cached 200 can't produce a false 'reachable'; assert each probe URL differs.
what_breaks_naive_approach: At the captive-portal boundary navigator.onLine is true but the backend is unreachable; the naive guard passes and the fetch silently fails or hangs, so scans queue indefinitely with no automatic flush and no user signal.

title: Design an op-based replication log with compaction for an offline-first task manager (Go backend)
business_context: A cross-platform task PWA with a Go/gRPC backend syncs by sending full task snapshots. As users accumulate 10k+ tasks, each sync re-transfers megabytes and the server can't tell what actually changed, so push notifications fan out spuriously. The team wants an append-only op log with compaction to make sync incremental and auditable.
constraints: Sync transfers only ops since the client's last position
Op log must be compactable so it doesn't grow unbounded
Ordering total per user; ops idempotent on replay
gRPC streaming for live ops; catch-up via paged history
10k tasks, 100k ops per user; compaction keeps log bounded

before_state.description: Sync sends full task snapshots; the server diffs nothing and notifies on every sync.

issues: Full snapshot transfer regardless of changes
No change detection; notifies on every sync
No ordering/idempotency; replays duplicate state

reasoning: Naive approach: store updatedAt per task and send tasks changed since a timestamp - rejected because timestamps don't give a total order across concurrent writers and can't express deletes or reordering cleanly. Alternative: full event-sourcing with no compaction - rejected because 100k ops/user grows unbounded and catch-up gets slower forever. Chosen path: an append-only op log keyed by a per-user monotonic position; clients stream from their position and catch up via paged history; a compactor periodically folds ops into a snapshot + truncates below the global min client position. Cross-layer trace: client emits op -> server appends with next position -> live subscribers get it via gRPC stream -> offline clients catch up from position -> compactor snapshots + truncates -> tests cover replay idempotency + compaction safety.

after_state.description: Mutations append to a per-user op log with a monotonic position; clients sync from their position (stream + paged catch-up) and a compactor folds old ops into a snapshot, keeping the log bounded.

files.purpose: Append-only, totally-ordered op log with paged catch-up reads from a client position.
Streams catch-up then live ops from the client's position; notifications ride real ops only.
Folds acknowledged ops into a snapshot and truncates the log below the slowest client, bounding growth.
Client resumes from snapshot+position and applies ops idempotently, persisting its position.

migration_strategy: Add op log + position columns; dual-write ops alongside the existing snapshot store
Backfill: synthesize an initial snapshot per user at the current state, position 0
Ship StreamOps + client streamSync behind flag op_log_sync to internal users
Canary 5%; compare bytes-transferred and notification counts vs snapshot path
Enable compactor with conservative min-position; ramp to 100% and retire snapshot Sync RPC
Rollback: flag off -> clients use snapshot Sync; op log keeps appending so re-enabling resumes cleanly
testing_strategy: Fixture: append 1,000 ops for a user; a client at pos 0 streams and assert (output) it receives them in order ending at pos 1000 and (side-effect) its IndexedDB matches a server fold. Idempotency: re-send the last 50 ops; assert alreadyApplied skips them and state is unchanged. Compaction/chaos test: with one slow client stuck at pos 200, run the compactor and assert TruncateOpsBelow uses 200 (not the head), so the slow client can still catch up; then advance it and re-run and assert further truncation. Negative: a client requesting a position below the truncation point is served the snapshot first.
what_breaks_naive_approach: At sync time the full-snapshot RPC transfers everything and notifies unconditionally; with 10k tasks this is megabytes per sync and a notification storm, and there's no way to send only what changed.

title: Add periodic background sync with constraints for an IoT dashboard PWA on metered connections
business_context: An agricultural-IoT PWA (React) shows farmers sensor readings. It currently refreshes only when open; a daily summary push relies on the user opening the app, so 40% of farmers miss the morning irrigation alert. The team wants Periodic Background Sync to refresh data in the background but must respect metered rural data plans and battery.
constraints: Use Periodic Background Sync where supported; graceful fallback elsewhere
Do not sync on metered connections unless user opted in
Respect a minimum interval (e.g. 12h) and skip if recently synced
Battery-aware: skip when device is low and not charging
Must update the cached summary and fire a local notification on new alerts

before_state.description: Data only refreshes on app open; no background mechanism, so background alerts never arrive.

issues: No background refresh; alerts missed when app closed
No metered/battery awareness
No local notification on new alerts

reasoning: Naive approach: register a setInterval to fetch in the background - rejected because timers don't run when the PWA is closed and would also ignore metered/battery constraints. Alternative: rely on server push (Web Push) for everything - rejected because the farmers want fresh dashboard data cached for offline viewing too, not just a notification, and Push alone doesn't refresh the cache. Chosen path: register Periodic Background Sync with a minInterval, gate execution on Network Information (metered) + Battery status + a last-sync guard, update the cache, and fire a local notification on new alerts; fall back to focus-refresh where unsupported. Cross-layer trace: app registers periodicSync('summary') -> SW periodicsync event checks constraints -> fetch summary -> diff for new alerts -> update Cache -> showNotification -> tests cover metered skip and battery skip.

after_state.description: A constrained Periodic Background Sync refreshes the summary in the background respecting metered/battery/last-sync guards, updates the cache, and notifies on new alerts; unsupported browsers fall back to focus refresh.

files.purpose: Registers periodic sync with a 12h minInterval and wires a focus fallback for unsupported browsers.
Background refresh gated by constraints; updates cache and notifies only on genuinely new alerts.
Encapsulates metered/battery/last-sync rules so background work respects rural data and power.

files.content:
export async function setSyncOnMetered(v){ await idbSet('pref:syncOnMetered', v); }
// surfaced in settings UI; defaults to false (data-plan friendly)
files.purpose: Lets farmers opt into metered background sync; default off protects their data plan.

migration_strategy: Ship focus-refresh fallback first (works everywhere), no behavior change for unsupported browsers
Add periodicSync registration behind flag periodic_sync for supported browsers + granted permission
Canary 10%; verify background refreshes occur and metered/battery guards skip correctly
Add new-alert notification; ramp to 100% of supported clients
Tune minInterval from engagement data; keep fallback path
Rollback: flag off -> unregister periodicSync, retain focus fallback so data still refreshes on open
testing_strategy: Fixture (SW harness): mock connection.type='cellular' with syncOnMetered=false and assert (output) refreshSummary returns early and (side-effect) fetch('/summary') is NOT called. Opt-in test: set syncOnMetered=true and assert it does fetch and writes the cache. New-alert test: seed prev summary with alertIds [1,2], return alerts [2,3], assert showNotification called once for alert 3 only. Battery chaos: mock battery {level:0.1, charging:false} and assert sync skipped; then {charging:true} and assert it proceeds.
what_breaks_naive_approach: A background setInterval simply does not execute while the PWA is closed, so the morning alert never refreshes; and even when running it would fetch on metered rural connections, burning data the farmer didn't consent to.

title: Reconcile optimistic UI updates with server-assigned IDs to fix broken references after offline create
business_context: A CRM PWA (Next.js) lets reps create a company and immediately attach contacts/notes to it while offline. The app assigns a temp client id; when the company syncs the server returns a real UUID, but the already-created child contacts still point at the temp id, so after sync ~4% of contacts become orphaned (attached to a non-existent company).
constraints: Children created offline must re-point to the server id after parent sync
Ordering: parent must sync before children referencing it
Temp ids must be globally distinguishable from server ids
No duplicate parents if the create is retried
References may be deep (note -> contact -> company)

before_state.description: Client temp ids are used for foreign keys; after the parent syncs to a real id, children keep the temp id and break.

issues: Children reference temp parent id permanently
No remap after parent gets a server id
Retried create could make two companies

reasoning: Naive approach: after parent sync, scan all children and string-replace the temp id - rejected as O(n) per sync and fragile across deep reference chains and multiple stores. Alternative: block child creation until the parent is online-created - rejected because it breaks offline-first (rep can't add contacts in the field). Chosen path: maintain an id-mapping table (temp->server) populated on parent sync, push children with their temp parentRef plus the mapping so the server resolves FKs, and rewrite local references transactionally; idempotency keys prevent duplicate parents. Cross-layer trace: create writes temp id + records in idmap -> push parent first (topological order) -> server returns real id -> idmap updated -> children pushed with idRefs resolved server-side and locally rewritten in one tx -> tests cover deep chains + retries.

after_state.description: A temp->server id map is populated on parent sync; the outbox is drained in dependency order, server resolves references via the map, and local FKs are rewritten transactionally; idempotency keys dedupe parents.

files.purpose: Distinguishes temp ids and provides a durable temp->server mapping for reference resolution.
Drains in dependency order, resolves references, persists the id mapping, and rewrites local FKs.
Atomically re-points all children to the server id and replaces the temp parent row.
Server mints the real id once per idempotency key so retries never create duplicate parents.

migration_strategy: Add idmap + outbox dependsOn metadata; new offline creates record dependencies
Ship topological drain + rewriteLocal behind flag id_remap to 5% of reps
Backfill any existing orphaned contacts by matching temp ids in historical outbox where possible
Canary: monitor orphaned-contact rate; ramp to 100%
Add idempotency table TTL sweep; remove naive string-replace path
