Rollback: flag off -> single-entity push (children may orphan as before) but outbox/idmap retained for re-enable
testing_strategy: Fixture: offline-create company (temp T1) then contact (companyId=T1) then note on that contact (deep chain). Drain with the server assigning real id R1. Assert (output) the company POST precedes the contact POST and (side-effect) after drain the contact.companyId === R1, the note resolves, and idmap has T1->R1, with zero rows still referencing T1. Duplicate test: retry the company POST with the same idemKey and assert only one server company exists. Chaos: 500 on the contact push mid-drain; assert order is preserved, the parent isn't re-created on retry, and no orphan results once it succeeds.
what_breaks_naive_approach: At sync the parent gets a server UUID but the already-created children keep the temp id; with no mapping or rewrite their foreign key points at a row that no longer exists, orphaning ~4% of contacts.

title: Add storage-quota management and graceful degradation when IndexedDB hits QuotaExceededError
business_context: A media-heavy real-estate PWA (Vue) caches listing photos and details for offline showings. On agents' older phones, IndexedDB hits the storage quota mid-write, throwing QuotaExceededError that the app doesn't catch, corrupting the half-written listing and crashing the showing view. ~8% of offline showings fail to load a listing.
constraints: Handle QuotaExceededError without corrupting partially-written data
Evict least-recently-used cached media to make room
Request persistent storage to reduce eviction by the browser
Critical text data must survive even when photos are evicted
No crash; degrade to text-only when storage is tight

before_state.description: Listings (text + photos) are written in one unguarded transaction; a quota error mid-write leaves a corrupt half-listing and throws to the UI.

issues: No quota handling; QuotaExceededError crashes the view
Text + photos in one tx; partial failure corrupts the listing
No eviction or persistent-storage request

reasoning: Naive approach: wrap the whole write in try/catch and ignore errors - rejected because the listing meta and photos share a transaction, so an error still leaves an inconsistent partial state and the agent can't view text either. Alternative: stop caching photos entirely - rejected because offline showings need images. Chosen path: split critical text from heavy media into separate transactions (text first, always succeeds), proactively manage quota with LRU eviction, request persistent storage, and on QuotaExceededError evict-and-retry then degrade to text-only. Cross-layer trace: cacheListing writes meta in its own tx -> requests persisted storage once -> writes photos with per-photo error handling -> on quota error runs LRU eviction and retries -> if still failing marks listing text-only -> tests cover quota + degradation.

after_state.description: Critical text is written first in its own transaction; media writes are quota-guarded with LRU eviction and retry; persistent storage is requested; the app degrades to text-only instead of crashing.

files.purpose: Writes text first (never corrupted) and guards each photo with eviction+retry, degrading gracefully.
Evicts photos from least-recently-used listings (keeping their text) to free space deterministically.
Requests persistent storage to reduce surprise browser eviction of cached showings.
Renders text always and only shows the gallery when photos are cached, never crashing.

migration_strategy: Split listings into text + photo stores via a non-destructive migration (existing rows get photosCached flag)
Add ensurePersistent() call on app start
Ship quota-guarded caching behind flag quota_safe_cache to 10%
Verify QuotaExceededError no longer crashes; measure offline-load success
Ramp to 100%; tune eviction target from telemetry
Rollback: flag off reverts to single-tx caching; split stores remain readable (text-first design is backward safe)
testing_strategy: Fixture: mock db.put to throw QuotaExceededError on the 3rd photo. Assert (output) cacheListing returns {degraded:true} and (side-effect) the listing text row exists with photosCached:false and no half-written corrupt photo remains. Eviction test: pre-fill with 5 cached listings, trigger evictLRU(30MB) and assert the least-recently-used listings' photos are deleted (oldest lastUsed first) until freed. Chaos test: throw QuotaExceededError even after one eviction; assert the app degrades to text-only and the UI shows the text fallback rather than throwing.
what_breaks_naive_approach: At the shared transaction the QuotaExceededError aborts mid-write, so the listing meta and photos are both rolled back or left inconsistent and the uncaught throw crashes the showing view - the 8% failure.

title: Move conflict resolution to a deterministic server merge function to stop client/server merge drift
business_context: A collaborative recipe PWA (Django + React) resolves edit conflicts on the client, but two clients running different app versions resolve the same conflict differently, so the server ends up with whichever client synced last - producing non-deterministic recipe content and ~3% of recipes oscillating between two states across syncs.
constraints: Conflict resolution must be deterministic regardless of client version
Server is the single arbiter; clients propose, server decides
Resolution must be reproducible (same inputs -> same output)
Backward compatible: old clients that resolve locally must be re-corrected
No lost edits: both sides' changes considered in the merge

before_state.description: Clients merge conflicts locally with version-specific logic and push the result; the server stores whatever arrives last, so different client versions disagree.

issues: Resolution logic lives on the client and differs by app version
Server stores last-arriving merge -> non-deterministic
Two clients can ping-pong different results forever
Server is a dumb store; no authoritative merge
Cannot reproduce a resolution; depends on client version

reasoning: Naive approach: pin all clients to the same resolution code via forced update - rejected because offline clients can't update and you still can't reproduce historical merges. Alternative: 3-way text merge on the server - rejected because recipes are structured (ingredients, steps, tags) and a text merge mangles structure. Chosen path: clients send their base + their changes (not a resolved blob); a single deterministic server merge function (pure, versioned) computes the authoritative result from base+ours+theirs and returns it; clients adopt it. Cross-layer trace: client sends {base, patch} -> server loads current -> computes mergeRecipe(base, current, patch) deterministically -> persists + returns canonical -> client replaces local -> tests assert same inputs always yield same output across runs.

after_state.description: Clients send base+patch; a pure deterministic server merge function arbitrates and returns the canonical recipe, eliminating version drift and oscillation.

files.purpose: Single deterministic, reproducible authority for resolving recipe conflicts server-side.

files.content:
def update_recipe(request, id):
    base = request.data['base']; patch = request.data['patch']
    with transaction.atomic():
        r = Recipe.objects.select_for_update().get(id=id)
        merged = merge_recipe(base, r.data, patch)
        r.data = merged; r.rev = r.rev + 1; r.save()
    return JsonResponse({'recipe': merged, 'rev': r.rev})  # canonical result back to client
files.purpose: Applies the deterministic merge under a row lock and returns the authoritative result.
Sends base+patch and adopts the server's canonical result so all clients converge identically.
Computes the client's intended changes relative to its base for the server to merge.

migration_strategy: Deploy server merge_recipe + base/patch endpoint; keep accepting legacy whole-blob PUTs temporarily
For legacy PUTs, server re-derives base from rev and runs merge_recipe to re-correct client-side merges
Ship base+patch client behind flag server_merge to 10%
Monitor recipe-oscillation metric to ~0; ramp to 100%
Remove legacy whole-blob acceptance after 30 days
Rollback: server still has merge; reverting clients to whole-blob is re-corrected by the legacy path, so no drift
testing_strategy: Fixture: base, current (server changed servings), patch (client added a tag + edited step 2). Assert (output) merge_recipe returns servings from current, the tag unioned, and step 2 from patch - and run it 100x asserting byte-identical output (determinism). Cross-version test: two different 'client merges' both reduced to base+patch produce the SAME server result. Chaos test: concurrent PUTs to the same recipe under select_for_update; assert both apply deterministically with no oscillation and rev increments monotonically with no lost tag.
what_breaks_naive_approach: At the push boundary the server stores whatever resolved blob arrives last; two client versions resolve the same conflict differently, so the recipe flips between their results on alternating syncs - the 3% oscillation.

title: Introduce a sync state machine with explicit phases to replace tangled boolean flags
business_context: A delivery-driver PWA (React) tracks sync with a handful of booleans (isSyncing, hasError, isOnline, pendingPush). Race conditions between these flags cause the app to start a pull while a push is mid-flight, occasionally double-applying server data and showing 'syncing forever' spinners; ~150 drivers/day get stuck requiring a force-quit.
constraints: Sync phases must be mutually exclusive (no overlapping push/pull)
All transitions explicit and logged for debugging
Stuck states must self-recover via timeout
Backward compatible with existing UI that reads a status string
Must handle online/offline transitions mid-sync

before_state.description: Multiple booleans tracked independently allow contradictory combinations (isSyncing && !isOnline), and transitions race so push and pull overlap.

issues: push() not awaited; pull races and double-applies
Contradictory flag combos possible (syncing while offline)
No timeout; a hung push leaves isSyncing=true forever (stuck spinner)

reasoning: Naive approach: add more guard booleans (isPushing, isPulling) - rejected because the combinatorial flag space keeps producing illegal states and races. Alternative: a third-party state library - rejected as overkill for a 5-state flow and adds bundle weight. Chosen path: an explicit finite state machine (idle->pushing->pulling->idle, plus error/offline) with a single current state, guarded transitions, transition logging, and a watchdog timeout that recovers stuck states. Cross-layer trace: UI reads machine.state -> trigger dispatches START -> machine moves idle->pushing (awaited) -> PUSH_DONE -> pulling (awaited) -> DONE -> idle; offline event forces ->offline; watchdog forces ->idle after timeout -> tests assert no overlap and recovery.

after_state.description: A finite state machine with one current phase enforces mutually-exclusive push/pull, logs every transition, recovers stuck states via a watchdog, and exposes a status string for the UI.

files.purpose: Single source of truth for sync phase with guarded, logged transitions - no illegal combos.
Awaits push fully before pull, eliminating the overlap that caused double-apply.
Self-recovers a hung push/pull after 30s, preventing the permanent 'syncing forever' spinner.
Adapts the machine to the legacy status string so the existing UI works unchanged.

migration_strategy: Introduce the machine alongside the booleans; derive booleans from machine state during transition
Route runSync through the machine behind flag sync_fsm at 10%
Verify no overlapping push/pull in logs and stuck-state recovery fires
Wire the watchdog; ramp to 100%
Delete the legacy boolean flags and their guards
Rollback: flag off reverts to boolean path; machine is additive so no data risk
testing_strategy: Fixture: drive the machine with START, PUSH_DONE, PULL_DONE and assert (output) the state sequence idle->pushing->pulling->idle and that an illegal PULL_DONE from 'pushing' is rejected (logged, no transition). Overlap test: call runSync twice rapidly; assert the second START is ignored (still single push then pull, side-effect: push called once per cycle). Chaos test: make push() hang; advance fake timers 30s and assert the watchdog dispatches FAIL moving to 'error', then RETRY recovers - no permanent spinner.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Because push() isn't awaited, pull runs concurrently and both apply server data, double-applying rows; and any contradictory flag combo or a hung push leaves isSyncing=true forever, stranding the driver in a spinner.

title: Add a Redis-backed sync session and resumable cursor to handle interrupted large initial syncs
business_context: An enterprise document PWA (NestJS + Redis + Postgres) does an initial sync of up to 250k documents on first login. On flaky networks the sync restarts from scratch each time it drops, so users on slow links never complete the initial sync (it loops for 40+ minutes) and 12% abandon onboarding.
constraints: Initial sync of 250k docs must resume from where it dropped, not restart
Server must track a per-session resumable cursor (Redis, TTL'd)
Pages must be stable under concurrent server writes during the long sync
Multiple devices per user each have an independent session
Memory-bounded server-side; no holding 250k rows in RAM

before_state.description: Initial sync pages with OFFSET and keeps no server-side position, so any disconnect restarts at offset 0 and re-sends everything.

issues: OFFSET paging shifts under concurrent inserts (skips/dupes)
No server-side session; a reconnect restarts at page 0
Client tracks page in memory; lost on tab reload

reasoning: Naive approach: have the client remember the last page number in localStorage - rejected because OFFSET paging is unstable under concurrent writes (rows shift, causing skips/duplicates) and a page number is meaningless after a write. Alternative: snapshot the entire result set server-side per session - rejected because materializing 250k rows in memory per session doesn't scale across many users/devices. Chosen path: keyset (seek) pagination on an immutable ordering (id) with a Redis-stored resumable cursor + session id (TTL'd), so any reconnect resumes from the last id regardless of concurrent inserts; client persists the session id. Cross-layer trace: client starts session -> server creates Redis session{cursor:0} -> each page seeks id>cursor -> updates cursor in Redis -> reconnect re-sends session id and resumes -> TTL cleans abandoned sessions -> tests cover disconnect resume + concurrent insert stability.

after_state.description: Keyset pagination over an immutable id with a Redis-backed, TTL'd session cursor lets a dropped initial sync resume exactly where it left off, stable under concurrent inserts.

files.purpose: Keyset paging + Redis cursor gives stable, resumable, memory-bounded server-side paging.
Persists the session id so a tab reload/reconnect resumes the initial sync from the server cursor.
Encapsulates session lookup; relies on Redis TTL to bound abandoned-session memory.

files.content:
-- ensure the keyset ordering column is indexed for fast id > cursor seeks
CREATE INDEX IF NOT EXISTS idx_docs_org_id ON docs(org, id);
files.purpose: Indexes (org, id) so each keyset page seek is fast even at 250k rows.

migration_strategy: Add (org,id) index and Redis session endpoints; keep the OFFSET endpoint live
Ship resumable client behind flag resumable_initial to 5% of new logins
Measure initial-sync completion rate and onboarding abandonment vs control
Ramp to 100%; remove the OFFSET endpoint after 30 days
Tune page size and Redis TTL from telemetry
Rollback: flag off reverts to OFFSET paging; Redis sessions TTL away harmlessly
testing_strategy: Fixture: 5,000 seeded docs; start a session and pull 2 pages, then simulate a disconnect (drop the connection) and resume. Assert (output) the resumed page continues at id>lastCursor with no duplicate or skipped doc and (side-effect) Redis cursor advanced monotonically. Concurrent-insert test: insert new docs with lower-than-cursor ids during paging and assert they don't shift the page window (keyset stability) vs an OFFSET control that would skip/dupe. Chaos test: expire the Redis session mid-sync (TTL); assert the client gets 404, restarts a session cleanly, and idempotent puts mean no data corruption.
what_breaks_naive_approach: On disconnect the OFFSET-based sync restarts at offset 0 and re-sends all 250k docs, and OFFSET shifts under concurrent inserts so pages skip or duplicate rows - making the initial sync loop for 40+ minutes and never converge on slow links.

title: Implement a grow-only counter CRDT for offline reaction counts in a streaming PWA
business_context: A live-events streaming PWA (React) lets viewers react (claps) during talks; on flaky venue Wi-Fi reactions are buffered offline. The current sync sends a final count per client and the server takes the max, so claps from multiple offline devices collapse to one device's number, undercounting engagement by ~35% and skewing the speaker leaderboard.
constraints: Counts from N offline devices must all add up, not collapse
Idempotent on retry: a re-sent batch must not double-count
Up to 200k concurrent viewers per event
Per-device offline buffer up to 10 min
Server stateless across 12 nodes; no global lock per reaction

before_state.description: Each client sends its local total; server stores max across clients, losing all but the largest contributor.

issues: Sends an absolute total; server max() collapses multiple devices
Retried flush double-counts or under-counts depending on server logic
No per-device attribution
max() is the wrong merge for additive counts
No per-device record; cannot reconcile concurrent contributions

reasoning: Naive approach: server adds the posted total each time (sum) - rejected because retries then double-count and a re-sync of the same device inflates the number unboundedly. Alternative: send each clap as a discrete event with a unique id - rejected because at 200k viewers that's a write storm and huge payloads on venue Wi-Fi. Chosen path: a G-Counter CRDT keyed by deviceId; each device reports its own monotonic subtotal, the server stores per-device maxima and sums them, so merges are idempotent and additive. Cross-layer trace: clap increments device-local counter -> flush posts {deviceId, count} -> server upserts max(count) for that device -> total = sum of all device maxima -> retries idempotent (max of same value) -> tests assert multi-device add-up + idempotent retry.

after_state.description: A G-Counter CRDT: each device contributes a monotonic per-device subtotal; the server stores the max per device and sums them, giving an additive, idempotent, conflict-free total.

files.purpose: Maintains a durable per-device monotonic clap count (the device's G-Counter slot).
Reports the device's own subtotal; safe to retry because the server takes the per-device max.
Stores per-device maxima and returns the additive sum; idempotent and lock-free across nodes.
Per-(talk,device) rows make the G-Counter durable and the sum trivially computable.

migration_strategy: Add clap_counters table; new posts write per-device while legacy max path still answers reads
Backfill: seed each known device's last reported total as its initial count
Ship per-device client behind flag gcounter_claps to one event as a pilot
Verify total equals sum of devices and survives retries; ramp to all events
Switch reads to sum(); remove the legacy max() path
Rollback: flag off reverts clients to absolute total; per-device rows remain and sum still correct
testing_strategy: Fixture: device A posts count=5, device B posts count=3 for the same talk. Assert (output) total=8 (additive) and (side-effect) two rows in clap_counters. Idempotency: re-post A's count=5 and assert total stays 8 (max no-op). Multi-device chaos: 50 devices each post increasing subtotals with random retries and reorderings; assert the final total equals the sum of each device's last value exactly, regardless of order (commutativity), and no device's contribution is lost.
what_breaks_naive_approach: At the server aggregation boundary max() over absolute device totals keeps only the largest device's number, discarding every other offline device's claps - the ~35% undercount.

title: Add an OR-Set CRDT for offline tag editing to fix add/remove conflicts in a DAM PWA
business_context: A digital-asset-management PWA (Angular) lets editors tag assets offline. The current set-merge unions tags, so when editor A removes a tag offline and editor B keeps it, the removal is lost on union and the tag reappears; conversely a naive intersection loses concurrent adds. Editors report 'zombie tags' on ~6% of assets, breaking search facets.
constraints: Concurrent add and remove of the same tag must resolve deterministically
Remove must win over a concurrent stale add but not over a newer add
Offline up to 7 days; up to 8 editors per asset
Tombstones for removed tags must be GC-able
Tag set per asset up to 500 tags

before_state.description: Tags are a plain string array; sync unions arrays, so removals vanish and the tag re-appears.

files.content: export function mergeTags(a, b){ return [...new Set([...a, ...b])]; } // union: removals lost
issues: Union ignores removals -> zombie tags
No causal info to distinguish a remove from a never-added tag
Concurrent add+remove of same tag is undefined

reasoning: Naive approach: track removed tags in a separate removed[] and subtract on merge - rejected because without unique add-tags a re-add after a remove is wrongly suppressed (remove永久 wins). Alternative: LWW per tag by timestamp - rejected because unreliable clocks make add/remove ordering wrong. Chosen path: an OR-Set (Observed-Remove Set) where each add carries a unique tag (uuid); remove tombstones only the observed add-tags it saw, so a concurrent fresh add survives a remove and a remove beats only the adds it observed. Cross-layer trace: add(tag) -> create {tag, uid} in adds -> remove(tag) -> move observed uids to tombstones -> merge unions adds and tombstones, element present iff it has a live (non-tombstoned) uid -> GC tombstones below watermark -> tests cover concurrent add/remove.

after_state.description: An OR-Set with unique add-tags: an element is present iff it has at least one add-tag not in the tombstone set; concurrent adds survive removes that didn't observe them.

files.purpose: OR-Set semantics so concurrent add/remove resolve correctly and merges are commutative.
Persists the OR-Set per asset and exposes intent-based add/remove/read APIs.
Server merges OR-Sets idempotently/commutatively; no locking needed across instances.
Bounds OR-Set growth by collecting tombstones once all clients have converged.

migration_strategy: Add tagsets store; migrate plain arrays to OR-Sets by add()-ing each existing tag once
Ship OR-Set client behind flag orset_tags to 5% of editors
Verify zombie-tag rate drops and search facets stabilize; ramp to 100%
Enable tombstone GC once client-observed watermarks are reported
Remove the union-merge path
Rollback: flag off -> client reads values() into a plain array; server keeps OR-Set so re-enable is lossless
testing_strategy: Fixture: asset with tag 'urgent' (uid u1). Editor A removes 'urgent' (tombstones u1) offline; Editor B concurrently re-adds 'urgent' (uid u2). Merge and assert (output) 'urgent' is PRESENT (u2 live) - the re-add survives. Inverse: A removes the only add it observed and B does nothing; assert 'urgent' is ABSENT (no zombie). Side-effect: tombs contains u1, adds contains u1,u2. Chaos: replay merges in random orders across 8 editors and assert the value set is identical (commutativity) and GC only drops fully-observed tombstones.
what_breaks_naive_approach: At merge the union re-introduces a tag that one editor removed (no record of the removal), and an intersection would instead drop a concurrent add - either way the tag set is wrong, producing zombie tags that pollute search facets.

title: Replace clobbering full-document sync with JSON Patch deltas and base-version guards for form drafts
business_context: A government-benefits application PWA (React) saves long multi-page form drafts offline. Sync currently uploads the whole 80-field draft; when a caseworker edits the same applicant's draft from a desktop, the mobile draft's full upload overwrites the desktop's field changes. ~9% of applications need rework because fields silently revert.
constraints: Sync must send only changed fields (deltas), not the whole draft
Concurrent edits to different fields must both persist
Conflicting edits to the SAME field must be flagged, not silently dropped
Base version must be validated server-side (optimistic concurrency)
Draft up to 80 fields, 30KB; deltas under 4KB

before_state.description: The whole draft is PUT on every sync; any field the other party changed is overwritten by the stale full document.

issues: Full-document PUT overwrites concurrently-edited fields
No base version; server can't detect concurrency
No conflict surfaced to the caseworker

reasoning: Naive approach: send only the dirty fields as a partial object and have the server Object.assign - rejected because without a base version the server can't tell a stale field from a fresh one, and two parties editing the same field still silently clobber. Alternative: full CRDT - rejected as overkill for a form with simple scalar fields and a clear 'flag conflict' UX requirement. Chosen path: compute a JSON Patch (RFC 6902) against the client's base version, send patch+baseRev; server applies only if each patched field still equals base, else records a field-level conflict for review. Cross-layer trace: edit marks field dirty -> on sync compute JSON Patch vs base -> PUT {patch, baseRev} -> server checks each op's field against base -> apply non-conflicting, flag conflicting -> return conflicts + new rev -> client resolves -> tests cover different-field merge + same-field conflict.

after_state.description: Sync sends a JSON Patch with the client's base revision; the server applies only fields unchanged since base and flags same-field conflicts for caseworker review.

files.content:
import { compare } from 'fast-json-patch';
export function buildPatch(base, current){ return compare(base, current); } // RFC6902 ops for changed fields only
files.purpose: Produces a minimal field-scoped patch (<4KB) instead of the whole draft.
Sends patch+baseRev and stores any returned field conflicts for the resolution UI.
Applies non-conflicting field ops under optimistic concurrency and reports same-field conflicts.
Surfaces same-field conflicts to the caseworker instead of silently dropping an edit.

migration_strategy: Add baseSnapshot+rev to drafts; backfill baseSnapshot=current data
Deploy patch endpoint while still accepting full-PUT for old clients (treated as patch vs empty base)
Ship patch client behind flag json_patch_drafts to 10%
Add conflict UI; ramp to 100% and monitor rework rate
Remove full-PUT acceptance after 30 days
Rollback: flag off reverts to full PUT; server still tracks rev so optimistic checks degrade safely
testing_strategy: Fixture: base draft with fields {income:100, address:'A'}. Mobile patches income->200; desktop already changed address->'B' (server rev advanced). Assert (output) the server applies income->200 and keeps address='B' (different fields both persist), zero conflicts, rev incremented. Same-field test: server changed income->150 while mobile patches income->200; assert a conflict {field:'income', server:150, client:200} is returned and income is NOT silently overwritten. Chaos: replay the same patch twice with the same baseRev; assert the second is a no-op (rev already advanced -> treated as already-applied) so no double mutation.
what_breaks_naive_approach: At the full-document PUT the stale mobile draft overwrites every field the desktop changed; the caseworker's edits silently revert, forcing rework on ~9% of applications.

title: Add Web Push with VAPID and offline-aware payload dedup for a logistics dispatch PWA
business_context: A courier dispatch PWA (Next.js) pushes new job offers. Couriers are often momentarily offline; the current push uses a non-standard polling fallback that fires duplicate job notifications when the device reconnects (it re-pulls everything), so couriers see the same job 3-4 times and accept stale jobs already taken. ~12% of accepts are for already-assigned jobs.
constraints: Standards-based Web Push (VAPID) with offline-tolerant delivery
Notifications must be deduped by job id across push + reconnect catch-up
A job already assigned must not be actionable from a stale notification
Subscription must survive SW updates
Payload encrypted; no PII in the push body

before_state.description: On reconnect the app re-pulls all open jobs and shows a notification per job, duplicating ones already shown via the prior poll.

issues: Polling re-notifies the same jobs on each cycle/reconnect
No dedup by job id
Stale jobs (already assigned) still notified and actionable

reasoning: Naive approach: keep polling but remember shown ids in memory - rejected because memory is lost on SW restart/reconnect, re-duplicating. Alternative: rely only on Web Push with no catch-up - rejected because pushes sent while the device is offline can be dropped by the push service, so couriers miss jobs entirely. Chosen path: VAPID Web Push for live delivery plus a reconnect catch-up that is deduped against a persisted 'shown job ids' store and validated against current job status, so neither path duplicates and stale jobs are non-actionable. Cross-layer trace: server sends push {jobId} via VAPID -> SW push handler checks shown-store -> shows once -> on reconnect catch-up fetches since-cursor, filters by shown-store + status -> tapping checks live status before accept -> tests cover dup + stale.

after_state.description: VAPID Web Push delivers live offers; a persisted shown-ids store dedupes against the reconnect catch-up; notification actions re-validate job status so stale offers can't be accepted.

files.purpose: Dedupes pushes by job id, uses a stable tag, and re-validates status on accept (409-safe).
Reconnect catch-up shares the shown-ids store with push so no job is notified twice.
Establishes a durable VAPID subscription so live pushes work; survives SW upgrades.
Sends encrypted VAPID pushes (no PII) and prunes expired subscriptions on 410.

migration_strategy: Stand up VAPID keys + subscribe endpoint; collect subscriptions behind flag webpush
Add shownJobs store + catch-up dedup; keep legacy poll as fallback for unsupported browsers
Canary one dispatch region; measure duplicate-notification and stale-accept rates
Ramp to 100% for push-capable browsers; legacy poll only for the rest
Add 410 subscription pruning; remove the legacy unconditional poll
Rollback: flag off -> poll-only; shownJobs store still dedupes within the poll path
testing_strategy: Fixture (SW harness): deliver a push for job J then run catch-up that also returns J. Assert (output) showNotification fires exactly once for J and (side-effect) shownJobs has one entry. Stale test: catch-up returns J with status 'assigned'; assert no notification. Accept-conflict test: tap accept on a job that the server now returns 409 for; assert the 'already taken' notification shows and no acceptance recorded. Chaos: deliver the same push twice (push service retry) and assert the tag + shown-store dedupe to a single visible notification.
what_breaks_naive_approach: On reconnect the poll re-pulls all open jobs and notifies each again with no persisted dedup, so couriers see the same job 3-4 times and act on stale, already-assigned offers - the 12% bad accepts.

title: Fix a SW update flow that strands users on a stale version with no controlled reload
business_context: A trading-desk dashboard PWA (React) ships fixes multiple times a day, but the service worker installs the new version and waits indefinitely (waiting state) because tabs never close on always-open trading screens. Traders run a stale build for days, missing a pricing-bug fix that caused ~$30k in mispriced orders before anyone noticed they hadn't updated.
constraints: New SW must activate without forcing traders to close all tabs
Must prompt before reloading (don't interrupt an in-flight order)
No update loop; reload exactly once per new version
In-flight WebSocket/state must be drained before reload
Critical hotfixes can be marked force-update

before_state.description: The new SW installs but stays in 'waiting' because clients never close; no skipWaiting, no update prompt, so traders run stale code.

issues: No skipWaiting; new version stuck in 'waiting'
No client notification of an available update
No controlled reload; traders run stale code indefinitely

reasoning: Naive approach: call self.skipWaiting() unconditionally in install - rejected because it activates immediately and a controllerchange can hard-reload the page mid-order, risking trade loss. Alternative: auto-reload on a timer when an update is detected - rejected because it can interrupt an in-flight order and risks reload loops. Chosen path: detect the waiting worker, prompt the trader, and on confirm postMessage SKIP_WAITING then reload exactly once on controllerchange after draining state; allow force-update for critical hotfixes. Cross-layer trace: registration finds reg.waiting -> app shows update toast -> on accept (or force flag) post SKIP_WAITING -> SW skipWaiting + clients.claim -> controllerchange -> drain WS/state -> single reload guarded by a flag -> tests cover no-loop + drain.

after_state.description: The app detects the waiting worker, prompts (or force-updates), drains in-flight state, then reloads exactly once via a guarded controllerchange handler.

files.purpose: skipWaiting only on explicit client message, so activation is user/force controlled.
Detects updates, reloads exactly once on controllerchange, and drains state before applying.
Drains WebSockets, flushes mutations, and persists UI so a reload doesn't lose in-flight work.
Prompts non-critical updates and auto-applies force hotfixes without interrupting normal flow.

migration_strategy: Ship message-driven skipWaiting SW + updateController behind flag controlled_update
Verify in staging that an update prompts and reloads exactly once (no loop)
Canary one desk; confirm in-flight orders drain before reload
Add force-update channel for critical pricing fixes; ramp to all desks
Remove the old install-only SW
Rollback: flag off -> SW reverts to waiting behavior; no skipWaiting message means no unexpected reloads
testing_strategy: Fixture (SW harness): install a new SW so reg.waiting is set; assert (output) watchForUpdates calls onAvailable with the waiting worker. Apply test: call applyUpdate and assert drainState ran (sockets closed, outbox flushed) before SKIP_WAITING posted, and that a single controllerchange triggers exactly one location.reload (guard prevents a second). Force test: set __HOTFIX__ and assert it applies without a prompt. Chaos: fire controllerchange twice rapidly and assert reload happens once; ensure an in-flight order present blocks reload until drain completes.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Without skipWaiting the new SW sits in 'waiting' behind always-open tabs forever, so traders run stale code for days and miss the pricing hotfix - the $30k mispricing.

title: Add request coalescing and SWR caching in the SW to cut redundant API load on reconnect storms
business_context: A news-reader PWA (Svelte) fetches article lists and bodies. When users return from offline, dozens of components each fire the same GET, and across 50k users reconnecting after a subway tunnel the backend sees a 10x read spike that has tripped rate limits and caused 503s. The team wants the SW to coalesce identical in-flight requests and serve stale-while-revalidate.
constraints: Coalesce concurrent identical GETs into one network request
Stale-while-revalidate for article bodies with a freshness window
Must not coalesce authenticated, user-specific responses incorrectly
Revalidation must be single-flight per URL
Bounded cache with TTL eviction

before_state.description: Every component fetch goes straight to the network; identical concurrent GETs all hit the backend, multiplying load on reconnect.

issues: No in-flight coalescing; N identical GETs => N network calls
No stale-while-revalidate; every view refetches
Reconnect storm multiplies backend load 10x

reasoning: Naive approach: cache-first with a long TTL - rejected because it serves overly stale news and still stampedes on expiry (cache stampede). Alternative: debounce in each component - rejected because components don't share state and it can't dedupe across independent callers. Chosen path: a SW-level in-flight promise map to coalesce identical GETs plus stale-while-revalidate that returns cached immediately and revalidates single-flight in the background; skip coalescing for authenticated responses via a Vary check. Cross-layer trace: fetch handler keys by URL -> if cached+fresh return it -> if cached+stale return stale + single-flight revalidate -> if uncached coalesce concurrent misses into one network promise -> populate cache with TTL -> tests cover coalescing + stampede.

after_state.description: The SW coalesces concurrent identical GETs into a single network request and serves stale-while-revalidate with single-flight background revalidation and TTL eviction.

files.purpose: Coalesces identical GETs and serves SWR so a reconnect storm hits the backend at most once per URL.
Bounds the SWR cache by evicting the oldest entries (TTL/LRU) to respect storage budgets.
Keeps app code simple; no per-component dedup needed since the SW owns it.

files.content:
self.addEventListener('message', (e) => { if (e.data === 'tidy') e.waitUntil(evictExpired()); });
self.addEventListener('activate', (e) => e.waitUntil(evictExpired()));
files.purpose: Runs eviction on activate and on demand to keep the cache bounded.

migration_strategy: Ship SWR+coalesce SW for the article/list endpoints behind flag swr_coalesce
Verify authenticated requests bypass the cache (no cross-user leakage)
Canary 10%; measure backend read QPS during a simulated reconnect storm
Tune freshness window + max entries; ramp to 100%
Add eviction scheduling; remove the passthrough fetch handler
Rollback: flag off -> passthrough fetch; cache simply goes unused
testing_strategy: Fixture (SW harness): issue 20 concurrent GETs to /api/articles with an empty cache. Assert (output) all 20 resolve with the body and (side-effect) the underlying fetch was called exactly once (coalescing). SWR test: pre-seed a stale cache entry; assert the request returns the stale body immediately and a single background revalidate updates x-cached-at. Auth test: a request with Authorization bypasses the SW cache entirely. Chaos/stampede: expire the entry and fire 50 concurrent requests; assert one network call (single-flight) and no 503-inducing fan-out.
what_breaks_naive_approach: On reconnect dozens of components each issue the same GET straight to the network with no coalescing; across 50k users that is a 10x backend read spike that trips rate limits and returns 503s.

title: Build an offline-capable shopping cart that reconciles price/stock changes at checkout for an e-commerce PWA
business_context: An e-commerce PWA (Next.js) lets shoppers build a cart offline on the train. The current flow trusts the cart's cached prices and stock at checkout, so when prices changed or items sold out while offline, ~5% of orders fail late in payment or honor wrong prices, causing refunds and margin loss averaging $7/order across 3,000 affected orders/month.
constraints: Cart edits persist offline; checkout reconciles against live price/stock
Price changes must be surfaced and re-confirmed, never silently honored
Out-of-stock items must be flagged before payment
Idempotent checkout; a retried submit must not create two orders
Reconciliation must be a single atomic server call

before_state.description: Checkout posts the cached cart with cached prices; the server bills cached prices and only discovers stock issues during payment capture.

issues: Honors stale cached prices
No stock check until payment, causing late failures
No idempotency; a retry can double-order
Server trusts client prices (also a security hole)
Stock not validated before charge
No idempotency key

reasoning: Naive approach: re-fetch prices on app open and update the cart - rejected because the shopper may be offline the whole trip and prices can change between open and checkout anyway. Alternative: lock prices at add-to-cart with a hold - rejected because inventory holds at scale across offline carts would oversell stock. Chosen path: a server reconciliation endpoint that, in one transaction, validates live stock and prices, returns a diff for the shopper to confirm, then a separate idempotent confirm/charge step using server prices only. Cross-layer trace: offline cart persisted -> on checkout POST /cart/reconcile -> server compares cached vs live price/stock -> returns diffs -> UI shows changes + re-confirm -> POST /checkout/confirm with idemKey using server-authoritative prices -> tests cover price change + OOS + retry.

after_state.description: Checkout first reconciles the cart against live price/stock in one atomic call, surfaces diffs for re-confirmation, then charges idempotently using server-authoritative prices.

files.purpose: Atomically reports live price/stock diffs so nothing stale is silently honored.
Reconciles, requires explicit confirmation on changes, and confirms idempotently with server prices.
Charges using server prices, decrements stock under lock, and is exactly-once via the idem key.
Shows the shopper exactly what changed so they re-confirm before any charge.

migration_strategy: Add reconcile + idempotent confirm endpoints; old checkout still works during transition
Switch client to reconcile-then-confirm behind flag reconcile_checkout at 5%
Measure late-payment failure and wrong-price rates vs control
Ramp to 100%; remove the legacy client-price checkout
Add idempotency TTL sweep; enforce server-price-only billing
Rollback: flag off reverts to legacy checkout; idempotency table is harmless if unused
testing_strategy: Fixture: cart with sku A (cachedPrice 10, live 12) and sku B (qty 3, stock 1). Assert (output) reconcile returns priceChanged for A and inStock:false for B, changed:true. Confirm path: after user accepts, /checkout/confirm bills server price 12 (side-effect: order.total uses 12, not 10) and rejects B with 409 oos. Idempotency/chaos: submit confirm twice with the same idemKey under concurrency; assert exactly one order, one charge, and stock decremented once (findForUpdate prevents oversell).
what_breaks_naive_approach: At checkout the server trusts cached client prices and skips a stock check, so it honors stale prices (margin loss) and only fails during payment capture when an item is sold out - the 5% late failures.

title: Replace timestamp-based delta sync with Lamport clocks to fix lost updates under clock skew
business_context: A multi-device habit-tracker PWA (React Native Web) syncs check-ins using device timestamps. Users' devices have skewed clocks (some set manually); a phone whose clock is 20 minutes fast makes its writes 'win' forever, so check-ins from a correctly-clocked tablet are silently dropped. Support sees ~200/week 'my streak reset' complaints.
constraints: Ordering must not depend on wall-clock correctness
A device with a fast/slow clock must not dominate
Causal updates fast-forward; truly concurrent updates merge by rule
Backward compatible with rows that only have a timestamp
Up to 5 devices per user, offline up to 30 days

before_state.description: Each write carries Date.now(); the server keeps the row with the larger timestamp, so a skewed-fast device's writes always win.

issues: Wall-clock ts decides winners; a fast clock dominates
Correctly-clocked device's updates dropped
No causality

reasoning: Naive approach: have the server stamp the time on receive instead of the client - rejected because offline writes arrive out of order, so receive-time mis-orders genuinely older edits ahead of newer ones. Alternative: NTP-sync the clients - rejected because we don't control device clocks and manual time settings persist. Chosen path: a Lamport logical clock per user; each write increments max(local, lastSeen)+1; the server orders by Lamport value with a deterministic device-id tiebreak, independent of wall-clock. Cross-layer trace: write reads local Lamport, increments, attaches -> server compares Lamport (not ts) -> higher wins, ties broken by device id -> server returns its Lamport so clients advance -> legacy ts rows treated as Lamport 0 -> tests cover skew domination + causal fast-forward.

after_state.description: Writes carry a Lamport clock; the server orders updates by logical time with a deterministic tiebreak, so clock skew no longer determines winners and causal updates fast-forward.

files.purpose: Maintains a durable Lamport clock and device id for skew-independent ordering.
Attaches the Lamport clock + device id and absorbs the server's clock on response.
Decides winners by Lamport value with a device-id tiebreak - independent of wall-clock skew.
Adds logical-clock columns; legacy ts-only rows default to Lamport 0 for backward compat.

migration_strategy: Add lamport/device columns defaulting to 0; reads unaffected
Deploy server Lamport comparison; legacy clients (ts only) mapped to lamport 0
Ship Lamport client behind flag lamport_sync to 10%
Measure 'streak reset' complaints; ramp to 100%
Remove ts-based comparison once all clients send lamport
Rollback: flag off -> clients send ts; server falls back to ts compare for lamport==0 rows
testing_strategy: Fixture: tablet (correct clock) writes value=true at lamport 5; phone (clock 20min fast) writes value=false at lamport 3. Assert (output) the tablet's write wins (higher lamport) regardless of ts, and (side-effect) the stored row has value=true. Causal test: device observes server lamport 10 then writes; assert its lamport >10 and fast-forwards. Tie/chaos test: two devices write at the same lamport; assert the deterministic device-id tiebreak picks the same winner on every node, and replaying the loser does not flip the result.
what_breaks_naive_approach: At the server comparison the larger wall-clock timestamp wins, so a device whose clock is 20 minutes fast permanently overrides correctly-clocked devices, silently dropping their check-ins and resetting streaks.

title: Add transactional outbox + change-data-capture so server-side writes reliably reach offline clients
business_context: A B2B SaaS approvals PWA (NestJS + Postgres + Kafka) pushes approval-state changes to clients' sync feeds. The server writes the DB row and then publishes to Kafka in a separate step; on crashes between the two, ~0.3% of approvals are committed in the DB but never published, so offline clients never learn an approval changed and act on stale data, causing duplicate approvals.
constraints: DB write and event publish must be atomic (no dual-write gap)
Events delivered to the client sync feed at-least-once, deduped client-side
Ordering preserved per aggregate (approval id)
Throughput 5k writes/sec; relay must not bottleneck
Backfill events from before the outbox existed must not be re-emitted

before_state.description: The service inserts the row then publishes to Kafka in a second call; a crash between them loses the event while the DB row persists.

issues: Dual write: DB + Kafka not atomic
Crash between writes loses the event (offline clients never learn)
No ordering or dedup guarantees

reasoning: Naive approach: publish to Kafka first then write the DB - rejected because a crash after publish but before commit emits a phantom event for a change that never happened. Alternative: 2-phase commit across Postgres and Kafka (XA) - rejected as operationally heavy and poorly supported. Chosen path: transactional outbox - write the domain change AND an outbox row in the same DB transaction, then a CDC/relay (Debezium-style or a polling relay) reliably publishes outbox rows to Kafka with the aggregate id as the partition key for ordering; clients dedupe by event id. Cross-layer trace: approve() writes row + outbox in one tx -> relay reads unpublished outbox in order -> publishes to Kafka keyed by approval id -> marks published -> client consumes feed, dedupes by event id -> tests crash between steps and assert no lost/phantom events.

after_state.description: Domain change and an outbox event are written in one transaction; a relay reliably publishes outbox rows in per-aggregate order; clients dedupe by event id - eliminating the dual-write gap.

files.purpose: Writes the state change and its event in a single transaction, removing the dual-write gap.
Reliably relays outbox rows in seq order, partitioned by aggregate for ordering and at-least-once delivery.
Durable outbox with an index for efficient unpublished scans and a sequence for global order.
Client dedupes by event id so at-least-once delivery is effectively exactly-once.

migration_strategy: Add outbox table + sequence; start dual-writing outbox rows inside existing transactions
Deploy relay reading only outbox rows created after a cutover seq (no backfill re-emit)
Run relay alongside legacy direct publish; compare delivered vs committed counts
Cut over consumers to the relay-fed topic; remove the direct kafka.publish call
Add seen-event dedup on clients; monitor lost/phantom event metrics to zero
Rollback: stop the relay and re-enable direct publish; outbox rows remain and re-relay on re-enable
testing_strategy: Fixture: call approve() and assert (output) one approvals row updated and (side-effect) exactly one outbox row in the same tx. Crash test: throw after the approvals update but before commit; assert NEITHER the row nor the outbox event persists (atomicity), so no lost or phantom event. Relay test: seed 3 outbox rows for the same approval and assert they publish in seq order with the aggregate key. Chaos/at-least-once: make the relay publish then crash before markPublished; on restart it re-publishes the same eventId and the client dedupes it (seenEvents) - no duplicate apply.
what_breaks_naive_approach: At the gap between the DB update and the Kafka publish a crash commits the approval but loses the event; offline clients never receive it and act on stale state, producing duplicate approvals.

title: Fix a cache-first SW that serves expired auth-gated API data and breaks token refresh
business_context: A SaaS time-tracking PWA (Vue) uses cache-first for /api/timesheets to feel fast offline. But cache-first also serves a 401 it once cached and never revalidates, so after a token expires users see stale timesheets and a silent failure to save; ~250 users/day are stuck viewing yesterday's data thinking it saved.
constraints: Auth-gated GETs must revalidate, not blindly serve cache
A cached error response (401/403) must never be served
Offline fallback to last good data only when truly offline
Token refresh must trigger on 401 and replay the request
Mutations must never be served from cache

before_state.description: Cache-first matches any cached response including a previously-cached 401, so the app shows stale data and never refreshes the token.

issues: Cache-first serves a stale or cached-401 response
Error responses cached and replayed
No token-refresh trigger; mutations could also be cached

reasoning: Naive approach: add a short TTL to cache entries - rejected because cache-first still serves a cached 401 within the TTL and doesn't address token refresh. Alternative: network-only for all /api - rejected because it kills the offline timesheet view users rely on. Chosen path: network-first for auth-gated GETs with offline fallback to last-good 2xx ONLY, never cache non-2xx, and a fetch wrapper that refreshes the token on 401 and replays; mutations bypass cache entirely. Cross-layer trace: fetch handler routes GET /api -> network-first -> on 2xx cache+return, on 401 refresh+retry, on network error return cached 2xx or 503 -> POST/PUT bypass cache -> tests cover cached-401 + offline fallback.

after_state.description: Auth-gated GETs are network-first, caching only 2xx; 401 triggers token refresh+replay; offline falls back to the last good response and mutations never touch the cache.

files.purpose: Network-first with 2xx-only caching, 401 refresh+replay, and offline fallback to last good data.
Refreshes the token on 401 and rebuilds the request so the replay carries the fresh credential.
Distinguishes a true-offline 503 from real data so the UI can show an honest offline badge.
Cleans up legacy cached 401/error responses left by the buggy cache-first SW.

migration_strategy: Ship network-first SW that caches only 2xx and purges cached errors on activate
Add 401 refresh+replay; verify token expiry no longer strands users
Canary 10%; confirm offline still shows last-good timesheets
Ramp to 100%; ensure mutations are network-only
Remove the legacy cache-first handler
Rollback: prior SW restorable, but activate-purge runs either way so no cached-401 lingers
testing_strategy: Fixture (SW harness): pre-seed the cache with a 401 for /api/timesheets; request it and assert (output) the SW does NOT serve the cached 401 and instead hits the network. 401-refresh test: first fetch returns 401, refresh returns a token, replay returns 200; assert refresh called once and the 200 body returned and cached. Offline test: network throws; assert it returns the last cached 2xx, not a 503, when one exists, else a 503. Chaos: cache a 500 then go offline; assert the 500 is never served (only 2xx fallback) and activate-purge removed it.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Cache-first matches a previously-cached 401 (or stale 2xx) and returns it without revalidating, so an expired token never refreshes and users see stale timesheets while saves silently fail.

title: Introduce a bounded retry budget with circuit breaker to stop offline queue from draining the battery
business_context: A ride-hailing driver PWA (React) flushes location pings and trip events from an offline queue. When the backend is down, the queue retries every item in a tight loop, pinning the CPU and draining driver phones from 100% to 20% in two hours, generating ~80 'app kills my battery' one-star reviews per week.
constraints: Bound total retry attempts/energy per window (retry budget)
Open a circuit breaker after consecutive failures; stop hammering
Half-open probe to recover automatically when backend returns
Critical events (trip end) prioritized over location pings
No event loss while the breaker is open (queue persists)

before_state.description: The drain loop retries every queued item immediately and repeatedly while the backend is down, busy-looping and burning CPU/battery.

issues: Tight infinite loop with immediate retries -> CPU/battery drain
No backoff or breaker when backend is down
No prioritization; pings block trip-end events

reasoning: Naive approach: add a fixed sleep between passes - rejected because a fixed sleep still hammers when down and is too slow when healthy. Alternative: drop events when the backend is down - rejected because trip-end events are revenue-critical and must not be lost. Chosen path: a token-bucket retry budget + a circuit breaker (closed/open/half-open) that stops retries when the backend is failing and probes to recover, with priority ordering so trip-end events go first; queue persists so nothing is lost. Cross-layer trace: drain consumes a retry token -> breaker closed: send, on N failures open breaker -> open: skip sends, schedule a half-open probe -> half-open: send one probe, success closes, failure re-opens -> priority queue orders trip-end ahead of pings -> tests cover battery-safety + recovery.

after_state.description: A token-bucket retry budget plus a circuit breaker halts retries while the backend is down, probes to auto-recover, and prioritizes critical events, keeping the queue durable.

files.purpose: Circuit breaker that opens on repeated failure and half-opens to probe recovery, stopping the busy loop.
Token-bucket retry budget caps retry frequency so the CPU/radio can't be pinned.
Drains under budget+breaker with priority ordering; stops cleanly when the backend is unhealthy.
Durable queue so events (especially trip_end) are never lost while retries are paused.

migration_strategy: Add breaker + budget around the existing drain behind flag retry_budget
Canary on a driver cohort; measure battery drain and CPU during a simulated backend outage
Verify trip_end events are prioritized and never dropped while open
Tune threshold/cooldown/budget from telemetry; ramp to 100%
Remove the tight-loop drain
Rollback: flag off reverts to the old loop; queue store unchanged so no loss
testing_strategy: Fixture: queue with 2 pings + 1 trip_end; make api.send fail 5x. Assert (output) the breaker opens after the threshold, drain returns early, and (side-effect) no further sends happen until the cooldown (use fake timers to verify the busy loop stopped). Priority test: assert trip_end is attempted before pings. Recovery test: after COOLDOWN a half-open probe succeeds -> breaker closes -> remaining items flush. Chaos/battery: count send attempts over 60s of downtime and assert they are bounded by the token budget (~not thousands), proving the battery drain is fixed.
what_breaks_naive_approach: When the backend is down the tight while(true) loop retries every item with no backoff or breaker, busy-looping the CPU and radio and draining the battery from 100% to 20% in two hours.

title: Implement selective sync scopes so a multi-tenant PWA only caches the active workspace offline
business_context: A project-collaboration PWA (Next.js) syncs ALL of a user's workspaces to IndexedDB. Power users belong to 40+ workspaces; the initial sync downloads 500MB and takes 6 minutes, frequently hitting storage quota and failing. The team wants selective sync that caches only the active workspace plus a small pinned set, evicting others.
constraints: Cache only the active + pinned workspaces, not all 40
Switching workspaces must lazily sync the new scope without a full re-download
Evict non-pinned, non-active workspaces under storage pressure
Per-workspace cursors so each scope syncs independently
Pinned workspaces always available offline

before_state.description: Initial sync pulls every workspace's data into one store with a single cursor, ballooning storage and time.

issues: Downloads all workspaces (500MB, 6min)
Single store + cursor; can't sync scopes independently
No eviction; hits quota and fails

reasoning: Naive approach: paginate the all-workspaces sync to avoid timeouts - rejected because it still downloads 500MB and exhausts quota. Alternative: never cache, always go online per workspace - rejected because offline access to the active workspace is the core requirement. Chosen path: scope-keyed stores with per-workspace cursors; sync only active+pinned scopes, lazily sync a scope on switch, and evict least-recently-used non-pinned scopes under storage pressure. Cross-layer trace: on workspace switch -> ensureScope(wsId) syncs that scope if absent -> per-scope cursor delta-syncs -> pinned scopes always retained -> storage pressure triggers eviction of LRU non-pinned scopes -> tests cover switch + eviction.

after_state.description: Each workspace is a sync scope with its own cursor; only active+pinned scopes are cached, switching lazily syncs a scope, and storage pressure evicts LRU non-pinned scopes.

files.purpose: Lazily syncs a single workspace scope with its own cursor, on demand.
Switching syncs only the target scope after freeing space - no full re-download.
Evicts LRU non-pinned, non-active scopes under storage pressure, keeping them re-syncable.

files.content:
export async function setPinned(db, wsId, pinned){ const s = await db.get('scopes', wsId) || { id: wsId }; await db.put('scopes', { ...s, pinned }); }
// pinned scopes are always synced on app start and never evicted
files.purpose: Lets users pin workspaces that must stay available offline regardless of pressure.

migration_strategy: Add scopes store + per-scope cursors; migrate existing items by tagging them with their workspace scope
Sync only the active + pinned scopes on next launch behind flag selective_sync
Add lazy scope sync on workspace switch; canary power users (40+ workspaces)
Measure initial sync time/size and quota-failure rate; ramp to 100%
Enable LRU eviction; remove the all-workspaces initial sync
Rollback: flag off reverts to all-workspaces sync; scope metadata is ignored harmlessly
testing_strategy: Fixture: user with workspaces A,B,C; pin A, active B. Launch and assert (output) only A and B items are in IndexedDB (C absent) and (side-effect) scopes store marks A,B synced. Switch test: switch to C; assert C lazily syncs and its cursor advances, while B remains. Eviction test: simulate storage at 80% quota and assert evictScopes deletes the LRU non-pinned scope (not A pinned, not the active one) and marks it re-syncable. Chaos: switch back to the evicted scope and assert it re-syncs from cursor 0 with no duplicate items.
what_breaks_naive_approach: The all-workspaces initial sync downloads 500MB and 6 minutes of data for a 40-workspace user, routinely exceeding the storage quota and failing the entire sync, leaving the user with no offline data at all.
